proposals.title: Learning Robust State Representations via Perceptual Bottlenecks: A Sensory Substitution Approach to RL from Pixels
proposals.motivation: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) agents that learn from raw pixels often rely on implicit, task-specific state abstractions learned by convolutional encoders, which can be brittle to distribution shifts and fail to generalize. This mirrors a core challenge in neuroprosthetics: creating robust internal models from unfamiliar sensory inputs. We propose that actively forcing an agent to learn from a transformed, information-preserving but non-standard perceptual stream (e.g., edge maps, spectral features) will compel the development of more foundational and generalizable state representations, moving beyond reliance on superficial pixel statistics.
proposals.hypothesis: Imposing a 'perceptual bottleneck' by transforming raw visual inputs into an alternative sensory modality (inspired by sensory substitution devices) will force RL agents to learn more robust, disentangled, and task-general internal state representations, improving sample efficiency and zero-shot generalization compared to standard pixel-based training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Deep Q-Network (DQN) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) architectures, replacing their standard convolutional input streams. Raw pixel observations from Atari and DeepMind Control Suite environments will be pre-processed into transformed modalities like Canny edge maps, Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) coefficients, or auditory spectrograms. The agent's encoder must learn to map this unfamiliar input to actionable policies. We will integrate apical dendrite-inspired mechanisms, where top-down task predictions modulate feature learning in the encoder, to encourage the development of abstract state variables. Performance will be benchmarked against standard pixel-based baselines on in-distribution and procedurally modified generalization tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for understanding and improving representation learning in RL by drawing direct inspiration from neuroscience. It will yield concrete evidence on whether forcing perception through an unfamiliar bottleneck enhances robustness. The findings could inform more data-efficient and generalizable RL agents, and offer a new experimental paradigm for probing learned representations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses data augmentation or auxiliary losses, the core novelty is the deliberate use of a *perceptually alien* but information-preserving input transformation to *force* representation learning, directly analogous to sensory substitution in humans. This approach is distinct from simple domain randomization and connects RL representation learning principles to neuroprosthetic adaptation.

proposals.title: Co-Evolving Game Mechanics and Policies via Self-Play in a High-Dimensional Design Space
proposals.motivation: Automated game design can accelerate creative discovery and test AI's capacity for complex system design. However, existing procedural content generation is often limited to low-dimensional, discrete parameter spaces, failing to capture the continuous, high-dimensional nuance of core game mechanics. This research addresses the challenge of searching vast, continuous design spaces for novel, balanced, and strategically deep games, a task intractable for manual human design.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing game design as a high-dimensional continuous control problem and optimizing it through competitive self-play will co-evolve novel, complex game designs alongside robust agent policies, with the emergent designs exhibiting greater strategic depth and balance than those from constrained, human-defined spaces.
proposals.method: We will implement a DDPG-based agent whose action vector directly parameterizes a wide array of continuous game mechanics (e.g., physics constants, reward functions, unit stats). This 'designer-agent' will play against a clone of itself in the game it generates. We will train the system using an iterative preference learning framework, inspired by recent reasoning work, where outcomes of self-play tournaments generate preferences over design parameters. These preferences will be used to update the designer-agent's policy, guiding it towards creating games that are 'learnable' yet non-trivial, effectively implementing a learned alignment objective between design and playability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for generative game design in high-dimensional continuous spaces and demonstrate the creation of games with emergent strategic properties. It will also contribute to the field of AI alignment by exploring how complex, human-relevant objectives (like 'fun' and 'balance') can be learned through competitive interaction and preference modeling, rather than hand-crafted reward functions.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct coupling of a high-dimensional continuous design space (typical of DDPG control domains) with the generative, open-ended search of self-play, moving beyond template-filling or symbolic generation to a truly generative and adaptive design process.

proposals.title: Scaling Continuous Control: A Dendritic and Apical Adaptation of DDPG for Ultra-High-Dimensional Action Spaces
proposals.motivation: Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) has proven effective for continuous control, but its scalability to domains requiring thousands of simultaneous, fine-grained actions (e.g., detailed character animation, complex robotic manipulation, or multi-agent systems) remains untested. This limitation hinders the application of deep RL to real-world problems with vast, continuous degrees of freedom, a critical frontier for embodied AI. Pre-2026 work on biological neural computation suggests potential architectural solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by incorporating structured, biologically-inspired neural architectures—specifically, models with apical and dendritic compartments for gated, context-dependent computation—the DDPG actor-critic framework can be scaled effectively to tasks with action spaces orders of magnitude larger than previously studied, overcoming the curse of dimensionality and credit assignment challenges.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DDPG algorithm by replacing the standard MLP actor network with a structured network featuring segregated dendritic and apical compartments, where apical inputs (e.g., from the critic or a task context module) modulate dendritic integration to enable sparse, factorized control signals. The critic will be augmented with Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for non-differentiable, long-horizon reasoning in complex environments. We will test this 'Dendritic-DDPG' on novel, high-dimensional benchmarks, including a simulated humanoid with thousands of muscle actuators and a multi-agent swarm control task, using iterative preference learning to refine the reward function from human feedback, ensuring societal alignment of the emergent behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish new performance benchmarks for DDPG in ultra-high-dimensional spaces and provide a novel, neuro-inspired architectural blueprint for scalable continuous control. It will also deliver a set of challenging, open-source environments to drive future research in this domain, bridging the gap between neuroscience-inspired AI and practical reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of compartmentalized neuron models (apical/dendritic) into a mainstream deep RL algorithm like DDPG to tackle dimensionality, a departure from simply scaling up width/depth. Furthermore, combining this with MCTS-based reasoning for credit assignment in such spaces is unexplored.

proposals.title: Inverse Dynamics Learning via Critic-Guided World Models
proposals.motivation: In reinforcement learning, learning an accurate model of environment dynamics is crucial for planning and sample efficiency but is often data-intensive and prone to compounding error. Pre-2026, value functions (critics) are trained to predict future rewards, implicitly encoding a rich, task-relevant understanding of the world's dynamics. This proposal asks if we can explicitly extract this knowledge to build better world models, directly addressing the inverse problem of inferring dynamics from a trained critic.
proposals.hypothesis: The state-action value function (Q-function) learned by a critic contains sufficient information to constrain and guide the learning of a generative forward dynamics model, leading to more sample-efficient and robust world models than those learned purely from state-action transitions.
proposals.method: We will leverage an actor-critic framework where a pre-trained, frozen critic network provides a learning signal. A separate dynamics model, potentially incorporating dendritic or apical computation for structured uncertainty, will be trained to predict next states. Its objective will combine a standard reconstruction loss with a novel consistency loss: the Q-value of the predicted next state (according to the frozen critic) must be close to the target Q-value of the true transition. This approach will be integrated with Monte Carlo Tree Search for active hypothesis testing, where the agent seeks transitions that maximize information gain for the dynamics model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for sample-efficient world model learning, demonstrate a new formal link between value functions and dynamics, and offer a pathway to more aligned agents by enabling them to build and reason with internal models that reflect task-specific consequences.
proposals.novelty: While inverse reinforcement learning infers rewards from policies, we propose inverting the critic to infer dynamics. This is distinct from standard model-based RL and novel in its direct use of the critic's gradient as a primary supervisory signal for dynamics learning.

proposals.title: Dual Actor-Critic Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Aligned Language Model Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with complex human values using static, scalar reward signals from human feedback is a fundamental bottleneck. Pre-2026, methods like RLHF rely on costly preference datasets that may not capture nuanced, multi-faceted objectives or generalize beyond the collected data. Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) from expert demonstrations offers a promising alternative to infer richer reward functions, but scaling it to high-dimensional language domains remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual actor-critic IRL framework, where one policy learns to imitate demonstrations and a separate critic infers a reward function, can recover more robust and generalizable alignment objectives than direct behavioral cloning or standard RLHF, leading to improved performance on held-out alignment tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the adversarial IRL framework to language, using a pre-trained LLM as the actor policy. A separate reward model (critic) will be trained to distinguish expert trajectories (high-quality, aligned responses) from actor-generated ones. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites for credit assignment, we will explore integrating dendritic computation modules into the critic's architecture to improve its ability to decompose and attribute reward to specific linguistic features. Learning will be stabilized using techniques from iterative preference learning (e.g., as in Monte Carlo Tree Search for reasoning) to generate informative counterfactual comparisons.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable IRL method for LLM alignment that infers dense, interpretable reward functions from demonstrations. It will provide empirical evidence on whether IRL-derived rewards offer advantages over standard reward modeling for out-of-distribution generalization and multi-objective alignment, advancing beyond the limitations of static preference datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit dual architecture for language IRL and the investigation of biologically-inspired (apical dendrite) credit assignment mechanisms within the reward model to handle the compositional nature of language, moving beyond treating demonstrations as mere imitation targets.

proposals.title: Continuous MCTS: Scaling Monte Carlo Tree Search to High-Dimensional Action Spaces via Learned Dendritic Policies
proposals.motivation: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) has proven transformative for reasoning and planning in discrete domains like Go and language. However, its application to continuous, high-dimensional action spaces—essential for robotics, complex control, and continuous game environments—remains a significant challenge. Scaling MCTS to these domains would unlock powerful search-based reasoning for a much broader class of real-world problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a learned, continuous policy network as a strong prior within MCTS, and leveraging apical dendrite mechanisms for credit assignment, can efficiently guide the search in high-dimensional spaces, making continuous-action MCTS both computationally tractable and performant.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MCTS framework by replacing the discrete action selection at nodes with a learned, amortized policy network that proposes continuous action candidates. This policy will be trained via iterative preference learning on trajectories generated by MCTS rollouts, creating a virtuous cycle of improvement. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites, we will implement a specialized credit assignment mechanism within the policy network to efficiently backpropagate sparse, search-derived rewards through the action proposal hierarchy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled algorithm for applying MCTS to continuous control and robotics tasks, directly extending the benefits of systematic search to these domains. It will also contribute a novel integration of dendritic computation with planning algorithms, offering new insights into credit assignment in hybrid neural-symbolic systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined policies with MCTS, the novel integration of iterative preference learning for policy refinement and a biologically-inspired dendritic credit assignment mechanism specifically for continuous action proposal is unexplored. This moves beyond simple discretization or random sampling approaches.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Multi-Agent Alignment via Apical Dendrite-Inspired Progress Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Training AI agents in complex, multi-actor environments remains a significant challenge for alignment and generalization. Pre-2026, static or uniformly scaled training curricula often lead to plateaus or catastrophic forgetting, especially when agents have heterogeneous capabilities. A principled method to dynamically sequence environments based on individual and collective learning progress is needed to efficiently steer multi-agent systems toward aligned, robust behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: That a curriculum which dynamically adjusts environment complexity for each actor, based on a measure of learning progress derived from apical dendrite-inspired internal signals, will lead to faster convergence, better final performance, and improved zero-shot generalization in multi-agent alignment tasks compared to static or random curricula.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework where a central curriculum manager uses Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to propose sequences of interactive environments. For each actor, learning progress will be quantified by analyzing the variance and error signals in a dedicated 'apical' module, inspired by cortical dendrites, that processes top-down context. This per-actor progress metric will guide the MCTS to select environments that maximize a combined objective of individual challenge and collective alignment, iteratively refining a preference model over curricula via preference learning from rollouts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete algorithm for dynamic, per-actor curriculum generation in multi-agent settings, grounded in neuroscientific inspiration. It will yield empirical results demonstrating improved sample efficiency and alignment in benchmark interactive environments, contributing a new perspective on using internal model dynamics for meta-learning.
proposals.novelty: The integration of biologically plausible apical dendrite signals as a core metric for curriculum generation is novel, moving beyond simple performance thresholds. Furthermore, applying MCTS-based reasoning to optimize a multi-objective curriculum across a population of heterogeneous agents, rather than a single agent, represents a significant advance over prior curriculum learning methods.

proposals.title: Partial Observability and Active Information Gathering: A Benchmark Suite and Dendritic Learning Approach for Agent Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current AI alignment and reasoning benchmarks predominantly assume perfect state observability, which is unrealistic for real-world embodied agents. Agents operating from high-dimensional sensory inputs (e.g., pixels) or in partially observable environments must actively gather information to form accurate world models and make aligned decisions. This gap limits the robustness and practical applicability of alignment techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating active information-gathering mechanisms, inspired by apical dendrite function, with iterative preference learning will enable agents to achieve better task performance and societal alignment in challenging, partially observable benchmarks than agents relying on passive observation.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel benchmark suite of 3D simulated tasks requiring hypothesis testing under partial observability (e.g., occluded objects, noisy sensors). Agents will be trained using a hybrid architecture combining a convolutional encoder for pixel inputs with dendritic network components to modulate hidden state updates based on task context, mimicking apical integration. An outer loop of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) will guide exploration for information gathering, with trajectories refined via iterative preference learning (as in recent reasoning work) against alignment criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new open-source benchmark for evaluating alignment under partial observability, 2) empirical evidence on the synergy between active perception and alignment learning, and 3) a novel agent architecture incorporating dendritic computation for robust latent state estimation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly bridging the fields of active perception (information gathering) and AI alignment within a single benchmark and algorithmic framework, moving beyond the perfect observability assumption common in prior alignment literature. The integration of dendritic computation for context-aware state estimation in this setting is also unexplored.

proposals.title: Evaluating and Improving Agent Alignment in Complex Multi-Agent Control via Apical Dendrite-Inspired Preference Learning
proposals.motivation: Current deep reinforcement learning benchmarks for continuous control, like those in MuJoCo or DM Control, are largely single-agent and focus on isolated physical skills. To develop agents that are robust and aligned in real-world, socially embedded settings, we need richer environments that test complex skill composition and multi-agent interactions, where alignment failures can have significant consequences. This expansion is critical for grounding societal alignment frameworks in concrete, interactive scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: That multi-agent continuous control tasks requiring complex, cooperative skills will expose novel failure modes in agent alignment, and that these can be mitigated by integrating iterative preference learning mechanisms, inspired by apical dendrite function, into the policy optimization process.
proposals.method: We will first design a suite of novel, physically simulated tasks in PyBullet or Isaac Gym that require long-horizon planning, tool use, and cooperative multi-agent objectives. We will then train agents using an actor-critic architecture augmented with dendritic computation models to segregate and integrate different reward and value signals. To align emergent behaviors, we will employ an iterative Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) process to generate trajectory candidates, which will then be scored by a preference model trained on human or synthetic feedback, following a process similar to Constitutional AI, to refine the policy via a KL-constrained objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A new open-source benchmark for complex multi-agent continuous control that stresses compositional reasoning and social interaction. 2) Empirical evidence on how architectural biases (like dendritic segregation) and algorithmic interventions (MCTS-based preference learning) can improve alignment in interactive, multi-agent settings. 3) A bridge between abstract societal alignment theory and concrete control problems.
proposals.novelty: The integration of biologically-inspired dendritic computation models with iterative preference learning for alignment is unexplored in continuous control. Furthermore, applying societal alignment frameworks to the low-level action space of multi-agent physics-based simulations represents a novel testbed for scalable alignment research.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite Credit Assignment for Sample-Efficient Hypothesis Testing in Interactive Environments
proposals.motivation: Foundation models deployed in interactive environments (e.g., robotics, scientific discovery) require vast numbers of costly trials to learn effective policies or test hypotheses. Current deep reinforcement learning and active learning methods are notoriously sample-inefficient, limiting their practical application in real-world settings where data collection is expensive or time-consuming. Improving sample efficiency is therefore a critical bottleneck for developing capable, generalist AI agents.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating biologically-inspired apical dendrite mechanisms for credit assignment with structured exploration strategies like Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) will enable more sample-efficient learning of world models and policies by improving the allocation of learning signals during interactive hypothesis testing.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel agent architecture where a neural network world-model features apical dendrite compartments, inspired by recent pre-2026 work, to modulate learning and credit assignment based on top-down contextual signals (e.g., a hypothesis to test). This model will be trained using a hybrid objective combining reconstruction, reward prediction, and a novel alignment loss derived from societal feedback datasets. For action selection and exploration, we will integrate an MCTS planner that uses the learned world model for simulation, with its outcomes refining a policy via iterative preference learning, directly reducing the need for environment interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new benchmark algorithm for sample-efficient interactive learning, validated in complex environments requiring hypothesis-driven exploration. It will provide empirical evidence for the utility of biologically-plausible credit assignment in large-scale AI systems. The resulting framework will demonstrate how alignment techniques and structured search can be synergistically combined to drastically reduce data requirements.
proposals.novelty: While MCTS and apical dendrites have been studied separately, their integration for sample-efficient hypothesis testing is unexplored. The novelty lies in using apical mechanisms to dynamically reconfigure credit flow during MCTS-driven simulation, creating a tight coupling between structured exploration and adaptive, context-sensitive learning.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Rapid Task Adaptation in Interactive Agents via Dendritic Computation and Preference Learning
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models and agents often require extensive fine-tuning or massive datasets to adapt to new task variations, limiting their efficiency and flexibility in dynamic environments. This is a critical bottleneck for deploying robust AI systems that can operate across diverse, real-world scenarios where tasks evolve. Enabling rapid adaptation from limited interaction is essential for advancing generalist agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent architecture combining meta-learning with dendritic computation for context-specific modulation can learn a policy that rapidly adapts to novel task variations within a suite using only a few episodes. Furthermore, we hypothesize that iterative preference learning, guided by Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), can efficiently shape this adaptation process.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-based meta-reinforcement learning agent. The core network will feature apical dendrite-inspired pathways, as explored in Lillicrap's recent work, to provide contextual, task-specific modulation of neural activity based on few-shot observations. The outer-loop meta-learning objective, using algorithms like MAML or Reptile, will train the base network and dendritic gating mechanisms to be sensitive to task changes. For adaptation, an inner loop will employ MCTS to explore action sequences in the new task variant, generating preference-ranked trajectories to update the policy via iterative preference learning, aligning adaptation with a reward model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel agent architecture that integrates meta-learning with biologically inspired dendritic computation for few-shot adaptation. It will demonstrate a practical methodology combining MCTS-driven exploration and preference learning for efficient in-context policy refinement. The results will provide new insights into building more flexible and sample-efficient AI systems capable of generalizing across task families.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of dendritic computation mechanisms for contextual gating within a meta-learning framework, and the use of MCTS-generated preferences to guide the few-shot adaptation loop, moving beyond standard gradient-based or black-box meta-learning approaches.

proposals.title: Contrastive Preference Learning: Shaping Latent Representations for Improved LLM Alignment and Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences remains a core challenge, often relying on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) which can be unstable and data-inefficient. Simultaneously, recent work suggests that the internal representations of these models are crucial for complex reasoning and interactive hypothesis testing. There is a need for methods that directly shape these latent spaces to be more structured and human-aligned, moving beyond scalar reward optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating contrastive learning objectives into the preference learning pipeline will produce better-structured latent representations in LLMs, leading to more robust alignment, improved sample efficiency, and enhanced performance on downstream reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will augment the Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and iterative preference learning framework from recent work with a contrastive loss (e.g., SimCLR or InfoNCE) applied to the model's internal representations (e.g., apical dendrite-inspired layers or final hidden states). Positive pairs will be generated from semantically similar responses or reasoning steps within the MCTS tree, while negatives will be drawn from divergent or low-reward trajectories. This contrastive objective will be combined with existing policy and value losses, encouraging the latent space to cluster solutions by conceptual similarity rather than just final reward.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for representation learning within alignment frameworks, potentially reducing the amount of preference data required. It will offer new insights into how latent space geometry influences reasoning and generalization in interactive environments, contributing to both the alignment and foundation model agent literature.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in self-supervised vision and language pre-training, its application to actively shape representations *during* iterative preference learning and MCTS-based reasoning for LLM alignment is unexplored. This bridges the gap between representation learning and interactive alignment paradigms.

proposals.title: Scaling World Models for Superhuman Hypothesis Testing: Integrating Apical Dendrite Mechanisms with Monte Carlo Tree Search
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models, while powerful, are limited as passive knowledge repositories. For true superhuman performance in complex, interactive environments, agents require a predictive world model of immense capacity to simulate and test long-horizon outcomes. Scaling such models is critical for alignment, as it enables internal simulation of societal and ethical consequences before action, a core challenge in pre-2026 AI safety research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling a model's capacity via novel, biologically-inspired neural components, combined with active inference mechanisms, will yield a world model capable of superhuman hypothesis testing and planning, thereby improving both capability and alignment.
proposals.method: We will scale a transformer-based world model by integrating apical dendrite mechanisms to enhance context-dependent computation and credit assignment, inspired by recent work on dendritic networks. This scaled model will be trained on massive, multi-modal datasets of interactive environments. To enable active hypothesis testing, we will use the scaled world model as a simulator within a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework, where MCTS iteratively proposes and evaluates action sequences, with outcomes refined through iterative preference learning on the model's own rollouts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scaled architecture for world modeling that demonstrates superhuman planning in complex simulations. It will provide a concrete framework for using scaled internal models for alignment, by pre-testing actions against societal outcome simulations. The integration of apical mechanisms with MCTS offers a new path toward sample-efficient, reasoning-based agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of scale and active inference: scaling not just parameters but architectural components (apical dendrites) for richer internal dynamics, and directly leveraging this capacity for MCTS-driven hypothesis testing, moving beyond passive scaling to create an active, reasoning world model.

proposals.title: Inverse World Model Learning: Inferring Environment Dynamics from Optimal Policies
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in AI alignment and safe deployment is understanding the implicit world model a learned policy has acquired. Current methods focus on training policies to succeed in known environments. The inverse problem—inferring the latent structure and dynamics of an environment from a successful policy alone—is crucial for interpretability, verifying that a policy's goals align with the designer's intent, and transferring policies to novel settings. This addresses a key gap in pre-2026 alignment research, moving from behavioral alignment to model-based understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: The structure and dynamics of a latent Markov Decision Process (MDP) can be probabilistically inferred from the state-action trajectories of a near-optimal policy, by framing the policy as providing a soft constraint on the space of consistent environment models.
proposals.method: We will treat the environment's transition dynamics and reward function as latent variables. Given a dataset of trajectories from a successful policy (e.g., from RL or learned from human feedback), we will perform approximate Bayesian inference over MDPs using variational methods. The policy will be modeled as approximately optimal under the inferred MDP, leveraging a modified Bellman optimality constraint as part of the variational objective. We will integrate dendritic computation models to allow neural network-based inference modules to separate and represent multiple plausible latent environment hypotheses, with Monte Carlo Tree Search used to actively generate informative queries for the policy to reduce ambiguity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical algorithms for the 'inverse RL for dynamics' problem, contributing directly to model-based interpretability and alignment. It will yield metrics for how uniquely an environment is determined by a policy and enable the transfer of policies by inferring the environment they were trained for. This advances beyond behavioral cloning to recovering the generative world model.
proposals.novelty: While inverse reinforcement learning infers rewards, inferring the full transition dynamics from a policy is a significantly under-constrained and less studied problem. The integration of active hypothesis testing via MCTS with dendritic networks for maintaining multiple latent hypotheses provides a novel, biologically-plausible computational approach to this inverse problem.

proposals.title: Learning Interactive World Models for Multi-Agent Alignment via Apical Dendrite Modulation
proposals.motivation: Current world models in reinforcement learning primarily focus on single-agent dynamics or treat other agents as part of a static environment. This fails to capture the interactive, intentional nature of multi-agent systems, which is crucial for developing AI that can align and cooperate with humans and other AI in shared environments. Progress in this area is essential for safe and effective deployment of AI in social or collaborative settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a world model architecture incorporating specialized circuits for modeling other agents' latent policies and goals, inspired by apical dendrite function for contextual modulation, will enable more accurate prediction of multi-agent interactions and facilitate the learning of aligned, cooperative behaviors.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based world model (e.g., of the Dreamer family) with separate prediction heads for environment dynamics and other agents' actions. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites for credit assignment, we will implement a gating mechanism where a top-down 'intentional' context, encoding predictions of other agents' goals (inferred via inverse reinforcement learning), modulates the bottom-up sensory stream to bias action predictions. Multi-agent trajectories will be generated using Monte Carlo Tree Search for planning, and the model will be trained on a dataset of interactive episodes, using iterative preference learning (as in recent reasoning work) to refine predictions towards aligned outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for multi-agent world modeling that explicitly reasons about other agents' intentions. It will provide a new benchmark for interactive prediction accuracy and demonstrate how such models can improve policy alignment in mixed-motive environments, bridging the gap between world model learning and societal alignment frameworks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating neuro-inspired apical dendrite mechanisms for contextual gating specifically for modeling other agents within a world model, and combining this with MCTS-based data collection and iterative preference learning to steer the model towards aligned interaction predictions.

proposals.title: One-Shot Task Induction: Meta-Learning and Alignment from a Single Example
proposals.motivation: Current meta-learning and in-context learning require substantial task-specific data, limiting their applicability in low-data regimes and obscuring the fundamental mechanisms of task acquisition. Pushing to the extreme limit of a single training example forces a direct confrontation with the core challenge of how a model can infer a general rule or objective from minimal evidence, a problem deeply relevant to robust alignment and efficient learning in interactive environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural networks equipped with structured, dendritic-like credit assignment pathways can perform meaningful meta-learning from a single example by rapidly forming and testing internal task hypotheses, and that this process can be guided and stabilized using preference-based learning objectives derived from the example itself.
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer-based architecture with dedicated apical dendrite-inspired pathways for fast, example-specific parameter modulation. The core algorithm integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for hypothesis generation over possible task rules implied by the single example. This search is guided by a reward function derived from the example's structure (e.g., consistency, simplicity). The model's final output is refined via iterative preference learning, where it generates multiple candidate responses to new inputs and learns to select those most consistent with the induced rule, creating a synthetic preference dataset from the single seed.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a formal benchmark and methodology for one-example meta-learning, providing insights into the minimal data required for task induction. It will contribute a novel architecture combining fast credit assignment with search-based reasoning, offering a path toward more sample-efficient and interpretable alignment techniques where objectives must be inferred from sparse human feedback.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its synthesis of MCTS-based internal hypothesis search with dendritic computation for one-shot learning, moving beyond gradient-based meta-learning and pure in-context learning. It frames single-example learning as an active information-gathering and preference-optimization problem, a perspective not explored in prior work.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Rapid Online Alignment of Language Models in Interactive Environments
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are typically aligned via extensive, static preference datasets, which limits their ability to adapt to new societal norms or user preferences in real-time. This is a critical bottleneck for deploying safe, helpful agents in dynamic, interactive environments where they must actively gather information and test hypotheses. A method for rapid, sample-efficient online alignment is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model, meta-trained on a distribution of alignment tasks (e.g., diverse preference datasets or reward functions), will develop internal representations and learning rules that enable rapid, few-shot adaptation of its policy to new human preferences or safety constraints during online interaction.
proposals.method: We will employ a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, where the inner-loop adaptation fine-tunes the model's policy (via a low-rank or apical dendrite-inspired parameter update) on a small batch of online interactions and preference feedback. The outer-loop meta-update will optimize the model's initial parameters across many simulated alignment tasks, each defined by a distinct reward model or preference dataset. We will test this in interactive text-based environments, using Monte Carlo Tree Search for exploration and to generate candidate responses for preference learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete framework for meta-alignment, demonstrating significant improvements in sample efficiency for online preference learning. It will provide empirical evidence on how meta-learning can instill robust, general-purpose adaptation mechanisms in LLMs, moving beyond static alignment. The findings could inform safer and more adaptable autonomous agent design.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to NLP tasks, its integration with online alignment—specifically combining MAML with iterative preference learning and active hypothesis testing in environments—is novel. This approach moves alignment from a one-time, dataset-centric process to a dynamic, model-inherent capability.

proposals.title: Bayesian Uncertainty for Robust Planning and Alignment in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) produce point estimates without reliable uncertainty quantification, which is critical for safe deployment in interactive environments and complex reasoning tasks. This lack of epistemic uncertainty hinders robust planning, hypothesis testing, and alignment, as models cannot distinguish between knowledge and guesswork.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Bayesian neural network principles into the transformer architecture will yield well-calibrated uncertainty estimates, enabling more reliable and data-efficient planning, exploration, and alignment through uncertainty-aware preference learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a scalable Bayesian LLM by applying Monte Carlo Dropout or a last-layer Laplace approximation to a pre-trained transformer. This model will be integrated into a planning loop using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), where uncertainty estimates will guide exploration. For alignment, we will extend iterative preference learning (e.g., DPO) to incorporate uncertainty, down-weighting high-uncertainty responses in the training dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for uncertainty-aware LLMs, directly improving the safety and sample efficiency of reasoning and interactive agents. It will offer novel methods for uncertainty-driven alignment, creating a bridge between Bayesian deep learning and societal AI safety frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian deep learning and LLM planning exist separately, their integration for uncertainty-guided MCTS and the application of apical/dendrite-inspired uncertainty to alignment mechanisms is unexplored. This moves beyond post-hoc calibration to core architectural changes for active inference.

proposals.title: Scalable Monte Carlo Tree Search via Learned Dendritic Policies for Combinatorial Action Spaces
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning and search methods, including Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), struggle with combinatorial or exponentially large action spaces, such as in complex strategy games, large-scale combinatorial optimization, or language generation from vast vocabularies. Scaling these methods requires new architectures for efficient action representation and selection, a core challenge for advancing AI in real-world domains. This directly addresses the 'scale up' theme in Lillicrap's recent work on active information gathering and reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a learned, structured policy network—inspired by apical dendrite computation for credit assignment—with a modified MCTS framework can enable efficient search and learning in environments with massive discrete action spaces by dynamically pruning and evaluating action subsets.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level architecture: (1) A 'dendritic policy' network that, given a state, generates a compact, factorized representation of the action space (e.g., specifying constraints or subspaces) rather than a flat distribution. (2) A modified MCTS that uses this representation to guide its search tree, expanding only promising action subspaces identified by the policy. The policy will be trained via iterative preference learning, using trajectories from MCTS rollouts as a synthetic preference dataset, aligning the policy's abstractions with long-term value.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel algorithmic framework for scaling search-based reasoning to previously intractable domains with combinatorial complexity. It will contribute a new neural architecture for action abstraction, bridging structured credit assignment ideas with large-scale planning. The results will advance the field of sample-efficient decision-making and offer new pathways for aligning AI systems in complex, open-ended environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit co-design of a biologically-inspired policy network for action space factorization with MCTS, moving beyond simple prior filtering. This integrates Lillicrap's themes of alignment (via learned preferences), apical dendrites (for structured credit), and scalable search in a novel way not present in prior MCTS or large-action-space RL literature.

proposals.title: Inverse Environment Inference: Recovering Latent Dynamics from a MuZero Agent's Policy and Representations
proposals.motivation: Understanding the environment rules an agent has learned is crucial for interpretability, safe deployment, and verifying alignment. Current methods focus on explaining agent behavior, not the inferred world model itself. This inverse problem, learning the environment from the agent, is a novel step towards auditing what foundational models believe about their operational context.
proposals.hypothesis: The latent dynamics and reward models learned internally by a MuZero agent contain sufficient, disentangled information to allow an external inverse model to reconstruct a high-fidelity, actionable approximation of the true environment's transition and reward functions.
proposals.method: We will train a MuZero agent in a known environment (e.g., a grid-world or simple game). We will then train an inverse inference network that takes as input the agent's learned representations (from its dynamics and prediction networks) and its action trajectories. This network will use an architecture with apical dendrite-inspired modules for conditional computation to separate and predict environment dynamics from reward signals. The model will be trained via iterative preference learning, comparing candidate rule reconstructions against the agent's actual policy and value predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and a practical tool for 'black-box' environment auditing of advanced agents. It contributes to the field of AI alignment by enabling tests of whether an agent's internal world model matches designer expectations. It also offers a new method for knowledge transfer between agents via inferred environment rules.
proposals.novelty: While prior work extracts policy or value functions, directly inverting a model-based RL agent's internal dynamics model to recover explicit environment rules is unexplored. The integration of apical dendrite-inspired networks for this structured prediction task is a novel architectural approach.

proposals.title: Modular Alignment via Dendritic Integration for Long-Context Multimodal Reasoning
proposals.motivation: End-to-end training of large multimodal models is computationally prohibitive and impedes modular updates or component reuse. A pre-2026 challenge is achieving robust, long-context reasoning across modalities (e.g., video+text) without full joint retraining, which is critical for scalable and adaptable AI systems. This work directly addresses the alignment problem of coordinating separate, pre-trained modules to perform as a coherent, integrated system.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular system, using biologically inspired apical dendrite-like mechanisms for cross-modal integration and iterative preference learning for alignment, can match the performance of end-to-end trained models on long-horizon multimodal tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a system from frozen, pre-trained vision and language encoders. Cross-modal fusion will be managed by a lightweight, trainable 'alignment network' implementing dendritic computation principles to gate and integrate information. The system will be trained using a variant of Monte Carlo Tree Search to generate candidate reasoning traces over long contexts, which are then refined via iterative preference learning (e.g., DPO) on a curated dataset of multimodal reasoning chains, forcing the alignment module to learn effective coordination.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a concrete framework for performant, modular multimodal AI that reduces computational costs and increases flexibility. It will also advance understanding of how to align independent components, a key challenge for AI safety and scalability, by drawing explicit connections between neural inspiration and machine learning methods.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines dendritic integration models—a recent focus in neural networks—with iterative preference-based alignment, a method from language model training, to solve the multimodal fusion problem without end-to-end gradients.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite-Driven World Models for Complex Non-Linear Dynamics via Iterative Preference Learning
proposals.motivation: Current model-based reinforcement learning and planning systems often rely on local linear approximations (e.g., in MuZero) that fail to capture complex, non-stationary dynamics in domains like robotic manipulation or multi-agent interaction. This limitation hinders sample efficiency and long-horizon reasoning. Developing more expressive, sample-efficient world models is a critical step toward scalable and aligned AI agents that can operate reliably in the real world.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating apical dendrite-inspired credit assignment mechanisms with iterative preference learning from Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) rollouts will enable learning of more accurate, globally coherent world models for highly non-linear dynamics, outperforming locally linear baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a recurrent world model architecture where apical dendrite-like structures modulate the credit assignment for long-term predictions, separating feedforward state updates from contextual, top-down targets. To train this model, we will generate a synthetic dataset of MCTS rollouts from an agent interacting with complex simulated environments. An iterative preference learning objective, akin to Direct Preference Optimization but applied to model predictions, will be used to refine the world model by ranking sequences of predicted states against ground-truth rollouts, forcing the model to capture global temporal consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neuro-inspired architecture for world modeling that explicitly addresses credit assignment over long time horizons. It will also provide a methodology for using agent-generated experience and preference learning for model refinement, advancing sample-efficient learning for planning. The resulting models should enable more robust planning in environments with sparse rewards and complex dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a biologically plausible apical dendrite mechanism for temporal credit assignment with a preference-based learning framework derived from MCTS reasoning traces, creating a new paradigm for training globally coherent world models without relying on local linearity assumptions.

proposals.title: Dendritic Replay and Preference Learning for Behavioral Cloning-Free Off-Policy Alignment
proposals.motivation: Behavioral cloning (BC) is a standard component for replay in off-policy reinforcement learning and language model alignment, but it inherently constrains policy improvement to the distribution of past data. This can limit exploration and the acquisition of novel, superior behaviors, a critical bottleneck for developing agents that can actively gather information and reason in complex environments. Challenging the necessity of BC could unlock more efficient and robust learning from diverse, suboptimal datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a replay mechanism based on iterative preference learning and apical dendrite-inspired credit assignment can effectively utilize off-policy data for policy improvement without a behavioral cloning objective, leading to better final performance and improved exploration capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and iterative preference learning framework from recent reasoning work to the domain of sequential decision-making. An agent will interact with an environment, storing trajectories in a replay buffer. Instead of BC, we will use MCTS to generate improved candidate trajectories from states in the buffer. A preference model, trained on these comparisons, will provide learning signals. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites for modulating learning, we will integrate a dendritic gating mechanism to selectively credit or ignore off-policy experiences based on their estimated utility for the current policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, high-performing algorithm for off-policy learning that does not rely on behavioral cloning, empirically demonstrating its advantages in sample efficiency and asymptotic performance. It will contribute a new perspective on societal alignment frameworks by showing how to learn from diverse human data without being constrained to mimic it exactly.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit removal of the behavioral cloning loss for replay, replacing it with a synergistic combination of MCTS-based trajectory improvement, preference learning, and biologically-inspired dendritic gating for selective credit assignment.

proposals.title: Active Information Gathering for Robotic Manipulation via Dendritic Predictive Models and Pixel-Based Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Current robotic manipulation policies often rely on perfect state observations, limiting their applicability to unstructured real-world environments where only high-dimensional sensory data (e.g., pixels) is available. Learning directly from pixels is a core challenge in embodied AI, but sample inefficiency and poor credit assignment in long-horizon tasks remain significant barriers. This work addresses the need for agents that can actively explore and gather information from raw sensory streams to solve complex manipulation problems.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating dendritic computation for predictive modeling with Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for planning will enable sample-efficient learning of manipulation policies directly from pixels, by allowing the agent to actively test hypotheses about object dynamics and improve its internal world model.
proposals.method: We will develop a reinforcement learning agent with a convolutional encoder that processes pixel observations. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites for credit assignment, we will incorporate dendritic networks to learn predictive models of environment dynamics and reward signals. This model will be used to guide an MCTS-based planner (extending ideas from iterative preference learning) to perform active information gathering, selecting actions that reduce uncertainty in the latent state for manipulation tasks. Training will use a combination of online interaction and a curated dataset of manipulation episodes.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute a novel architecture for pixel-based robotic control that explicitly models active information seeking. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and task performance on benchmark manipulation suites (e.g., Meta-World) compared to standard model-free and model-based RL from pixels. The work will also provide insights into integrating structured planning with learned dendritic representations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of dendritic predictive models—for improved credit assignment in sensory streams—with MCTS for active hypothesis testing, specifically tailored for learning manipulation from pixels. This moves beyond passive observation assumptions and standard model-based RL by having the agent actively query the environment to resolve perceptual ambiguity.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Skill Discovery via Intrinsic Motivation and Dendritic Computation for General-Purpose Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic manipulation relies heavily on task-specific reward engineering, which is brittle, non-scalable, and limits agents to narrow, pre-defined goals. For robots to operate effectively across diverse, open-world environments, they must autonomously acquire a broad repertoire of reusable skills without dense, human-specified rewards. This aligns with the broader challenge of aligning agent behavior with complex, underspecified human intent.
proposals.hypothesis: That an agent equipped with intrinsic motivation and structured neural computation can discover a diverse and useful set of manipulation skills in a task-agnostic manner, and that these skills will provide a robust foundation for efficient adaptation to downstream tasks specified by sparse rewards or language commands.
proposals.method: We will train a robotic agent in a simulated, physics-rich environment using a two-level intrinsic motivation system. A curiosity-driven, information-theoretic objective (e.g., empowerment or diversity-based) will drive exploration. This will be integrated with a neural architecture featuring dendritic compartments (apical dendrites) to separate and modulate intrinsic drives from later, sparse extrinsic task signals, as suggested by recent work on credit assignment in complex networks. The discovered skill embeddings will be evaluated by fine-tuning on a suite of Meta-World and Language-Table style benchmarks using iterative preference learning or Monte Carlo Tree Search for efficient policy refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for unsupervised skill acquisition in manipulation, reducing dependency on reward engineering. It will demonstrate how dendritic computation can facilitate the integration of multiple learning objectives, contributing to more general and aligned agents. The resulting skill library will serve as a testbed for studying efficient adaptation and compositional reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The integration of structured neural models (apical dendrites) with modern intrinsic motivation algorithms for robotic skill discovery is largely unexplored. Furthermore, linking this unsupervised pre-training phase directly to language-conditioned adaptation via iterative reasoning methods bridges the gap between foundational motor learning and high-level instruction following.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite Modulation for Integrating Human Priors into Active Hypothesis Testing
proposals.motivation: Foundation models that actively gather information in interactive environments, as explored in prior work, face a fundamental tension between autonomy and sample efficiency. Pure reinforcement learning is slow, while direct imitation of human demonstrations can limit exploration and final performance. This is critical for aligning AI systems in complex, real-world tasks where safe, efficient learning is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a biologically inspired mechanism, using apical dendrites as a modulatory channel, can effectively integrate sparse human demonstrations or engineered symbolic priors to guide and accelerate an agent's active hypothesis testing, without overriding its autonomous learning capabilities.
proposals.method: We will extend an active hypothesis-testing agent (e.g., based on Monte Carlo Tree Search for planning) with a cortical microcircuit model featuring apical dendrites on its policy network. Human demonstrations will be processed into a prior policy, and environmental rules will be encoded as symbolic constraints. These priors will generate modulatory signals targeting the apical dendrites of the network, biasing but not dictating neuronal activity. Learning will occur via a combination of preference learning from outcomes (as in recent MCTS-boosted reasoning) and TD-error-based updates to the basal dendrites, allowing the agent to override the priors with experience.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neuro-inspired architecture for hybrid learning, demonstrating how to combine explicit prior knowledge with autonomous discovery. It will provide a concrete method for improving the sample efficiency and initial alignment of agents in interactive environments, advancing towards more data-efficient and safer AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a specific biological computation (apical dendrite modulation) as a principled, non-destructive interface for priors within a modern active inference agent, moving beyond simple behavioral cloning or reward shaping.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite Modulation for Sample-Efficient Robot Learning via Language-Guided Monte Carlo Tree Search
proposals.motivation: Training deep reinforcement learning (RL) agents on real robots is severely bottlenecked by sample inefficiency, limiting practical deployment. Pre-2026 advances in model-based planning (e.g., Monte Carlo Tree Search) and biologically-inspired architectures (e.g., apical dendrite signals for contextual modulation) offer promising but largely separate paths for improvement. Integrating these with language-based abstractions could provide a unified framework for efficient learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep RL agent, whose policy network is modulated by apical dendrite signals carrying language-derived task abstractions, will demonstrate significantly improved sample efficiency when its exploration is guided by a learned model and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) in interactive environments.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent architecture where a language model processes task descriptions and interaction histories to generate a compact task embedding. This embedding will serve as an apical signal, modulating the forward pass of a policy network. The agent will use an ensemble of learned world models to perform MCTS for planning, with the apical context focusing the search. Off-policy data from MCTS rollouts and real interactions will be stored in a shared dataset for training the policy, value functions, and models via temporal-difference and model-based learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-plausible architecture for context-aware RL that tightly integrates high-level language guidance with low-level control. It will provide a concrete framework for using MCTS not just for reasoning but for direct sample-efficient policy improvement in robotics, validated on real robotic manipulation tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of language-derived apical modulation for network specialization with model-based MCTS for exploration, creating a synergistic loop where high-level abstraction guides low-level search and data acquisition, an approach not yet explored in pre-2026 robot learning.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Fast Adaptation of Robotic Manipulation Skills
proposals.motivation: Deploying robots in unstructured environments requires the ability to quickly adapt learned skills to new objects, dynamics, or tasks with minimal data. Pre-2026, meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) has shown promise for fast adaptation in simulation but struggles with sample efficiency and stable transfer to real-world robotic systems. This work addresses the critical gap between rapid meta-learning and reliable physical embodiment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-RL agent, augmented with a structured, biologically-inspired dendritic computation module, can learn a more generalizable and sample-efficient prior over manipulation skills, enabling significantly faster adaptation to novel tasks on a physical robot compared to standard meta-RL baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-RL algorithm based on recurrent architectures (e.g., RL^2) or gradient-based methods (e.g., MAML), enhanced by an apical dendrite-inspired neural network component. This module will process contextual information (e.g., object properties, goal descriptions) to modulate the plasticity of the policy network's hidden layers. We will train the agent on a diverse dataset of simulated manipulation tasks (e.g., from Meta-World) and evaluate fast adaptation performance on a real robot arm facing unseen objects and perturbed dynamics, using a small number of trial episodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel meta-RL architecture integrating dendritic computation for improved context-dependent adaptation, 2) an empirical demonstration of rapid skill adaptation on a physical robotic platform, and 3) a publicly released dataset of manipulation tasks designed for meta-learning benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a biologically-plausible apical dendrite mechanism—a theme from the researcher's recent work—into a meta-RL framework to address the stability and generalization challenges of current methods, moving beyond purely connectionist models.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite-Driven Hierarchical RL for Compositional Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to perform complex, long-horizon tasks like 'set a table' requires composing sequences of simpler skills over extended timeframes. Standard flat reinforcement learning (RL) policies struggle with the credit assignment and exploration challenges inherent in such sparse-reward, multi-stage problems. A hierarchical approach is necessary to bridge the gap between high-level planning and low-level motor control.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical RL agent, where a high-level manager leverages Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for sub-task planning and a low-level worker utilizes apical dendrite-inspired credit assignment for skill learning, will significantly outperform monolithic and standard hierarchical policies on long-horizon manipulation benchmarks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level hierarchy. The high-level manager operates on a learned latent space of sub-goals and uses MCTS to sequence them, informed by a learned world model to test hypothetical action sequences. The low-level worker is a policy trained with RL to achieve these sub-goals, but its learning is modulated by an apical dendrite-like signal from the manager, implementing a form of top-down credit assignment to stabilize training across sub-tasks. The entire system will be trained end-to-end on a dataset of manipulation trajectories, using iterative preference learning to refine the manager's planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, neurally-inspired hierarchical RL architecture that tightly integrates deliberative planning with efficient skill learning. It will provide a new benchmark for compositional task performance in simulation and demonstrate a principled method for credit assignment across temporal abstraction levels.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of MCTS-based planning for high-level sequencing with a biologically plausible apical dendrite mechanism for low-level policy guidance, creating a unified framework for exploration and credit assignment that is not present in prior hierarchical RL or option-critic architectures.

proposals.title: Inverse Reward Inference via Apical Dendritic Modulation in Learned Policies
proposals.motivation: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) is crucial for aligning robot behavior with human intent, but traditional IRL is computationally expensive and struggles with complex, high-dimensional state spaces. Recent advances in deep reinforcement learning have produced powerful Q-functions and policies, yet methods to efficiently invert these models to recover interpretable reward functions remain underdeveloped. This gap limits our ability to audit, correct, and align learned behaviors, especially in interactive environments where robots must test hypotheses about human preferences.
proposals.hypothesis: The structured representations within a deep neural network's policy or Q-function, particularly those influenced by apical dendrite-like mechanisms for contextual modulation, encode sufficient information to efficiently infer a sparse, underlying reward structure. We hypothesize that leveraging these internal representations will yield more sample-efficient and interpretable inverse reward inference than model-free IRL approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we train a robot policy using deep RL (e.g., SAC or PPO) on a dataset of demonstrations, incorporating a computational model of apical dendrite influences to encourage structured, context-sensitive representations in the penultimate network layer. Second, we treat the trained policy's internal activations as a state embedding and frame reward inference as a linear inverse problem regularized for sparsity, using the Bellman optimality constraint. We will benchmark against classic IRL and maximum entropy IRL on simulated robotic manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computationally efficient algorithm for inverse reward inference that directly leverages the internal structure of modern deep RL policies. It will advance the field of AI alignment by providing a tool to audit and interpret the reward structures implicitly learned by agents. Furthermore, it will offer new insights into how biologically-inspired architectural motifs, like apical dendrites, can be harnessed for more transparent and controllable machine learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty lies in explicitly using the contextual representations shaped by apical dendritic computation—a recent focus in neural network research—as the basis for inverse reward inference, rather than treating the policy as a black-box input-output function. This approach connects specific architectural inductive biases to the interpretability of learned behaviors.

proposals.title: Active System Identification for Robust Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) often assumes a perfect or high-fidelity simulator, limiting its application to real-world, uncertain environments. Drawing a direct analogy from control theory's system identification, this project addresses the core challenge of learning an accurate dynamics model purely through interaction. This is critical for deploying RL agents in complex, safety-critical domains like robotics or healthcare where the true dynamics are unknown and must be discovered online.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing environment interaction as an active system identification problem, where an agent strategically explores to reduce model uncertainty, will lead to more data-efficient and robust MBRL policies compared to methods that treat model learning as a passive byproduct of task exploration.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent that maintains an ensemble of probabilistic neural network dynamics models to capture epistemic uncertainty. Inspired by recent work on Monte Carlo Tree Search for reasoning and apical dendrites for credit assignment, we will design a dual-objective policy. This policy will use an upper-confidence-bound planner (like PETS) that balances task reward maximization with an intrinsic reward for reducing model uncertainty in state-action regions identified by the ensemble's predictive variance. The learning process will be iterative, refining the model and policy in tandem from a collected interaction dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled MBRL framework that explicitly unifies optimal experiment design from system identification with policy optimization. It will provide a clear methodology for sample-efficient learning in environments without a simulator. The resulting analysis will offer new insights into the alignment between an agent's learned world model and the true environment dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in rigorously adapting the formal, iterative hypothesis-testing loop of control-theoretic system identification into the MBRL loop, moving beyond treating model error as a static loss to an actively managed quantity. This creates a direct bridge between classical adaptive control theory and modern deep RL, a connection underexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Evolving an Ecological Niche of Adversaries: Curriculum Learning for Robust and Generalist Agents
proposals.motivation: Self-play, while powerful, risks producing agents that are overspecialized to their own evolving strategies, leading to brittle policies that fail against novel opponents or in non-stationary environments. This is analogous to the lack of robustness seen in some aligned language models when faced with distributional shifts or adversarial inputs. A method that systematically cultivates diverse training challenges is needed to improve generalization and robustness in competitive and cooperative AI agents.
proposals.hypothesis: That an adversarial curriculum, driven by sampling from a strategically maintained and diverse 'ecological niche' of opponent strategies, will yield agents with faster learning, greater robustness, and better generalization compared to those trained solely via naive self-play.
proposals.method: We will implement a training ecosystem comprising: 1) a population of the agent's past checkpoints, 2) strategically generated counter-strategies via a search-based method (e.g., Monte Carlo Tree Search), 3) human demonstration data, and 4) policies from other algorithms (e.g., DQN, PPO). An 'apical' curator module, potentially using learned value functions or diversity metrics, will sample from this niche to propose training opponents. The agent's policy, enhanced with dendritic network components for richer state representation, will be trained via iterative preference learning against this curriculum, where preferences are derived from learning progress signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired framework for adversarial training that moves beyond simple self-play. It will deliver a concrete algorithm for ecological niche sampling and demonstrate its empirical benefits in benchmark environments, contributing to the fields of robust multi-agent learning and AI alignment by promoting generalist capabilities.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formalization and maintenance of a diverse adversary ecosystem as a curriculum driver, explicitly preventing overspecialization. This integrates concepts from population-based training, curriculum learning, and adversarial robustness in a unified framework inspired by ecological principles.

proposals.title: Beyond Self-Play: Improving Agent Robustness and Learning Speed via Diverse Opponent Sampling
proposals.motivation: Self-play has driven breakthroughs in competitive multi-agent RL (e.g., AlphaGo, OpenAI Five), but it risks developing idiosyncratic strategies that overfit to a single, co-evolving opponent. This can limit robustness against novel strategies and slow learning in complex, open-ended environments. Investigating alternative training regimes is crucial for building more generally capable and aligned agents that can operate in diverse real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an agent against a diverse population of opponents, rather than only against its latest self, will lead to faster learning and more robust policies that generalize better to novel adversaries.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and preference learning framework from recent work to a multi-agent setting. A main agent will be trained via policy gradient, with its value function refined through MCTS rollouts against a fixed pool of diverse opponents. This pool will include past versions of the main agent (self-play), intentionally constrained agents, and agents trained with different reward functions or network architectures (e.g., utilizing apical dendrite-inspired modules for different credit assignment). Learning progress and opponent diversity will be managed via an iterative preference learning mechanism that prioritizes challenging and informative opponents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the benefits and limitations of diverse opponent training compared to pure self-play. It will contribute a practical framework for managing opponent populations in complex RL and offer insights into how strategic diversity influences robustness and alignment properties in competitive agents.
proposals.novelty: While population-based training exists, our approach uniquely integrates MCTS-based reasoning and iterative preference learning to actively curate a diverse opponent set, moving beyond simple past checkpoints or hand-crafted adversaries to dynamically generate a curriculum of challenging partners.

proposals.title: Multi-Population Adversarial Self-Play Grounded by Realism Priors for Robust Robotic Skill Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Self-play and adversarial training have shown promise for learning complex behaviors, but in domains like robotics, they often produce policies that exploit simulation flaws or converge to degenerate, non-physical strategies. This simulation-to-reality gap and lack of strategic diversity limit the robustness and generalizability of learned policies. Grounding the adversarial process with a realism prior is a critical, unsolved challenge for scaling these methods to real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-population adversarial framework, where agent teams compete and collaborate, will generate a diverse curriculum of strategies, and scoring interactions with a learned realism discriminator will constrain this search to physically plausible behaviors, leading to policies that are more robust and transferable to the real world.
proposals.method: We will implement a league of multiple agent populations that compete in teams, with a central archive storing past policies for diverse match-ups. A key innovation is a realism discriminator network, trained on a limited dataset of real-world robot interactions or high-fidelity simulation data, which will assign a physical plausibility score to state-action trajectories. This score will be integrated as a penalty in the reward function. The training will utilize policy gradient methods, with strategic diversity encouraged through population-based training and a preference learning objective, inspired by recent advances in iterative preference optimization, to rank policies based on both task performance and realism.
