proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for integrating domain knowledge (via realism priors) into open-ended adversarial learning, directly addressing the sim2real gap. It will yield a method for generating robust and diverse robotic policies without extensive real-world trial-and-error. The resulting multi-population archive will also serve as a rich source of varied strategies for analysis and rapid adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a learned realism discriminator as a dynamic reward shaper within a multi-population adversarial league is novel. Unlike static dynamics randomization, this actively penalizes non-physical strategies emerging from self-play, creating a grounded curriculum that is co-evolved with the agents' policies.

proposals.title: Self-Play Reinforcement Learning with Apical Dendrite Targets for Complex Real-World Policy Search
proposals.motivation: Self-play reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved superhuman performance in discrete, rule-based games like Go and chess. However, scaling these methods to complex, continuous, and high-dimensional real-world domains like robotics control or protein conformation search remains a significant challenge. These domains lack clear win/loss conditions and require learning from sparse, delayed rewards, which current self-play frameworks struggle to optimize effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-play framework, augmented with a learned preference model trained on iterative Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) rollouts and guided by apical dendrite-inspired targets for credit assignment, can discover robust and generalizable policies in complex, continuous domains where traditional RL fails.
proposals.method: We will adapt the AlphaZero-style self-play loop to a continuous action space. An agent and adversary will be instantiated as twin neural networks. To guide exploration and credit assignment in sparse-reward settings, we will integrate an apical dendrite-inspired mechanism, where top-down targets from a learned value/policy network modulate the plasticity of lower-level sensory-motor pathways. A preference model, trained iteratively on outcomes from MCTS rollouts (as in recent reasoning work), will provide a dense learning signal by ranking trajectory segments, replacing binary win/loss signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable self-play algorithm validated on high-fidelity robotic manipulation or protein folding simulation benchmarks. It will demonstrate how principles from neuroscience (apical dendrite integration) and iterative preference learning can be combined to solve complex real-world RL problems. The resulting framework will advance the field towards more autonomous systems capable of mastering open-ended physical and scientific tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a biologically-inspired apical control mechanism within a self-play loop, using a learned preference model from MCTS rollouts to provide a dense training signal for continuous control, moving beyond discrete game environments.

proposals.title: Bootstrapping Expert-Aligned Policies via Verifier-Guided Self-Play
proposals.motivation: Pure self-play reinforcement learning (RL), while powerful, can be sample-inefficient and may converge to strategies that are non-intuitive or misaligned with human preferences. Conversely, imitation learning from expert data is limited by the quality and quantity of available demonstrations. A hybrid approach that efficiently leverages a small expert seed to guide and accelerate self-play could address these limitations, particularly for complex games or real-world tasks where expert data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: A verifier model, trained on a limited expert dataset to distinguish expert-like from non-expert trajectories, can effectively filter self-play data to create a high-quality, bootstrapped training corpus. This process will yield a policy that learns faster, adheres more closely to expert style, and ultimately surpasses the performance ceiling of the seed data.
proposals.method: We will train an initial policy network via behavioral cloning on a small dataset of expert game trajectories. In parallel, we will train a separate verifier model (e.g., a binary classifier or preference model) on the same data to predict 'expert-likeness'. The initial policy will then engage in self-play, generating a large volume of trajectories. The verifier will filter these, selecting only high-scoring trajectories to be added to a hybrid training dataset. This dataset will be used to iteratively refine the policy via on-policy RL (e.g., PPO) or offline RL, with potential integration of Monte Carlo Tree Search for improved planning, creating a bootstrapping loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for semi-supervised RL that combines the sample efficiency of imitation learning with the exploratory power of self-play. It will demonstrate a method for 'aligning' an RL agent's exploration with a human-expert prior without extensive online human feedback. The resulting algorithm aims to set a new benchmark for learning performance in data-scarce, strategically complex environments.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit use of a separately trained verifier as a static filter to curate self-play data, an approach inspired by bootstrapping techniques in computational biology but distinct from typical reward modeling or preference learning in RLHF, which often requires ongoing human comparison data.

proposals.title: Apical Dendritic Integration for Hybrid Self-Play: Combining Sparse Expert Data with Monte Carlo Tree Search for Language Model Alignment
proposals.motivation: Pure self-play alignment, while powerful, can lead to reward hacking and distributional collapse, while purely human-supervised fine-tuning is costly and limited in scale. A hybrid approach that integrates limited, high-quality human data with scalable self-play could offer a more robust and sample-efficient path to aligning language models with complex societal values. This addresses a core challenge in AI alignment: ensuring systems remain corrigible and beneficial when operating beyond their initial training distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training regimen combining a small, curated dataset of expert human judgments with a self-play loop guided by Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) will lead to language models that are more robustly aligned, generalize better to novel scenarios, and exhibit less reward gaming than models trained with either method alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MCTS-boosted reasoning framework from recent work to generate and explore candidate model responses in an interactive 'society simulator' environment. A key architectural innovation is the use of a learned apical dendritic integration mechanism, where a separate network module processes the sparse expert data to provide a modulating signal (a prior or a bias) to the policy network's apical dendrites during the MCTS rollouts and policy updates. This integrates the static expert knowledge dynamically into the iterative search and preference learning process.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, neurally-plausible algorithm for hybrid alignment training and provide empirical evidence on the synergy between expert data and self-play. It will advance the methodology for building AI systems that can actively test hypotheses about human values in novel environments while being anchored by limited but reliable human knowledge.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration mechanism—using apical dendritic computation to modulate a self-play policy based on static data—and in framing MCTS not just for reasoning but as an active information-gathering process for alignment, directly guided by a compact human knowledge base.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Closing Competency Gaps: An Automated Machine Teaching Framework for Adaptive Self-Play Curriculum
proposals.motivation: Self-play is a powerful paradigm for training agents in competitive and cooperative settings, but learning often plateaus due to static or poorly sequenced training distributions. Pre-2026, curriculum learning and population-based training require extensive manual tuning or lack a principled mechanism to diagnose an agent's specific weaknesses. An automated system that actively diagnoses and targets competency gaps could dramatically improve sample efficiency and final performance in complex domains like language model alignment and strategic reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: An automated machine teaching meta-controller, which diagnoses learner deficiencies via performance and gradient analysis and synthesizes targeted training scenarios, can generate a more efficient self-play curriculum than static or random opponent sampling, leading to faster learning and higher asymptotic skill.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component system: a Diagnoser and a Synthesizer. The Diagnoser, inspired by Monte Carlo Tree Search for reasoning and apical dendrite models for credit assignment, will analyze the agent's loss landscape and failure modes across a held-out test set of environments. The Synthesizer, leveraging a generative model trained on a diverse dataset of environment parameters and opponent strategies, will produce new training episodes that maximize the expected learning gradient norm for the diagnosed gap. This closed-loop system will be evaluated in multi-agent reinforcement learning benchmarks and a simplified language-based negotiation task.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework for automated machine teaching within self-play, providing concrete algorithms for real-time curriculum optimization. It will demonstrate empirically how targeted adversarial environment generation can accelerate learning and lead to more robust agents, with implications for aligning AI systems through adaptive training.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration of a diagnostic module, using detailed gradient and failure analysis, with a generative synthesizer to create bespoke training challenges. This moves beyond population-based or scheduled curricula to a fully adaptive, on-demand teaching process that directly targets the learner's current weaknesses.

proposals.title: Apical-Dendritic World Models for Curriculum-Guided Self-Play in Unknown Environments
proposals.motivation: Autonomous agents must learn to act in complex, initially unknown environments. Current methods for learning world models and policies often rely on random or exhaustive exploration, which is inefficient and can fail to discover critical dynamics. A principled, adaptive curriculum for exploration, grounded in the agent's own learning progress, is needed to enable efficient and robust mastery of novel domains.
proposals.hypothesis: That an agent can co-learn a world model and a policy more efficiently by using the uncertainty estimates from a dendritic neural network architecture to dynamically generate a self-play curriculum, which sequences exploratory challenges from known to uncertain state transitions.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent with a world model/policy network featuring apical dendrite-inspired modules to separately compute prediction and uncertainty. A Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) planner will use this model to generate exploratory trajectories. A curriculum module will analyze model uncertainty and policy competence to select starting states for self-play, initially from high-likelihood regions and progressively from areas of high uncertainty or learning progress, creating a tailored training dataset. This loop of model learning, curriculum generation, and policy improvement via iterative preference learning on successful trajectories will be iterated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for sample-efficient world model learning through introspective curriculum design. It will demonstrate how biologically-inspired neural computation can guide exploration, and offer a new method for aligning agent exploration with learnable complexity without external reward shaping.
proposals.novelty: The integration of apical-dendritic uncertainty mechanisms directly into a dynamic, model-based curriculum generator for self-play is novel. Unlike prior curriculum learning, the curriculum here is explicitly driven by the agent's internal model uncertainty and competence, creating a closed-loop system for autonomous skill acquisition.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Belief Inference for Rapid Adaptation in Novel Imperfect-Information Games
proposals.motivation: While agents like DeepStack and Pluribus have achieved superhuman performance in specific imperfect-information games like poker, they require immense, game-specific training. This limits their applicability to the vast space of real-world strategic interactions with hidden information. A general agent capable of quickly adapting to novel, related games would be a significant step towards more flexible and sample-efficient AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-reinforcement learning agent, trained on a distribution of imperfect-information games, can learn to rapidly adapt its core strategic modules—including its belief-state inference and opponent modeling components—to achieve competent performance in a new, unseen game from the same family.
proposals.method: We will use a recurrent architecture (e.g., an LSTM or Transformer) as the agent's policy and value network, enabling it to maintain and update an internal belief state. The meta-learning objective will be Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), applied to the parameters of this network and a dedicated opponent-modeling head. The agent will be meta-trained on a procedurally generated distribution of simplified, partially observable games (e.g., parameterized Liar's Dice, small-scale poker variants). Adaptation will be evaluated via few-shot performance on held-out game configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete framework for meta-learning in multi-agent, imperfect-information settings, demonstrating fast adaptation. It will provide empirical evidence on which neural components (e.g., belief RNNs, opponent model weights) are most crucial to adapt. The results will advance the goal of creating generally capable strategic agents that operate under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying meta-learning not just to the policy, but explicitly to the belief-state inference and opponent modeling mechanisms that are central to imperfect-information reasoning, moving beyond prior work focused on perfect-information or single-agent meta-RL.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Fast Policy Adaptation in Interactive Environments via Apical Dendrite Modulation
proposals.motivation: Foundation models struggle to adapt efficiently to novel, structured environments like games with new rules, requiring extensive new data and fine-tuning. This limits their ability to act as generalist agents that can quickly align their behavior to new constraints or objectives. Fast adaptation is crucial for safe alignment and for testing hypotheses in interactive settings, as highlighted in recent work on societal alignment and active information gathering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which optimizes a model's initial parameters for rapid gradient-based adaptation, can be significantly enhanced by incorporating a biologically-inspired, context-dependent modulation signal via apical dendrites to specialize network pathways for new game rules.
proposals.method: We will extend MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) by integrating a context-encoding network that processes a description of the new game rules. This context vector will modulate the forward pass of the main policy network via apical dendrite-inspired multiplicative interactions in specific layers, enabling fast specialization. The system will be meta-trained on a distribution of procedurally generated grid-world games, and evaluated on its ability to adapt within few episodes compared to standard MAML and fine-tuning baselines, using Monte Carlo Tree Search for planning during adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neuro-inspired architecture for meta-reinforcement learning that enables faster and more sample-efficient adaptation to new environmental rules. It will provide a concrete framework for studying how compositional structure in tasks can be leveraged for alignment and rapid hypothesis testing in agents.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of meta-learning optimization with a structured, biologically-plausible modulation mechanism (apical dendrites) for in-context adaptation, moving beyond standard weight updates. This addresses a gap in using dedicated neural circuitry for fast, context-dependent policy specialization in reinforcement learning.

proposals.title: Cross-Game Feature Transfer for Accelerated and Aligned Agent Learning
proposals.motivation: Training capable agents from scratch in complex environments is data-inefficient and risks the development of misaligned, reward-hacking policies. Transfer learning, a cornerstone of modern AI, remains underexplored for transferring learned representations and behavioral priors between distinct but structurally related games, which could address both efficiency and alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: Initializing an agent's network with features learned from a source game will significantly accelerate learning and improve final-task alignment in a target game, provided the games share abstract relational structures that can be captured by apical dendrite-mediated credit assignment.
proposals.method: We will train a base agent using PPO or an actor-critic variant with auxiliary prediction losses on a source game (e.g., a grid-world with object interactions). The convolutional and recurrent network layers will be transferred to initialize an agent for a target game (e.g., a different grid-world with shifted rules). We will specifically investigate freezing versus fine-tuning apical dendrite-inspired pathways, which modulate cortical pyramidal neuron activity, to control the plasticity of transferred high-level features. Performance and alignment will be benchmarked against agents trained from scratch.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for cross-domain skill transfer in reinforcement learning, demonstrating concrete sample efficiency gains. It will also contribute to the alignment literature by showing how pre-training on environments with well-specified reward functions can instill beneficial priors that reduce reward hacking in novel settings.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is common, the explicit use of game-theoretic structural similarity as a transfer criterion and the mechanistic investigation of apical dendrite pathways for preserving or modifying abstract relational knowledge in RL agents is novel.

proposals.title: Learning Composable Abstractions via Dendritic Bottlenecks in Procedurally Generated Environments
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale models achieve generalization through massive compute and data, obscuring the principles of abstraction formation. Understanding how to learn fundamental, transferable concepts under strict compute constraints is crucial for developing more efficient, interpretable, and aligned AI systems. This addresses core challenges in sample efficiency and compositional reasoning highlighted in pre-2026 alignment and foundation model research.
proposals.hypothesis: A system with architectural inductive biases for factorization, trained on a curriculum of procedurally generated environments sharing core mechanics, will learn composable abstractions that enable zero-shot transfer to novel game families, and these abstractions will be explicitly reportable by the model.
proposals.method: We will design a neural network with modular components and explicit bottlenecks, inspired by apical dendrite mechanisms for contextual gating. The agent will be trained via reinforcement learning with an iterative preference learning objective, akin to a learned reward model, on a curriculum of simple 2D game families (e.g., resource gathering, spatial navigation) where mechanics are systematically varied. Knowledge factorization will be enforced through architectural constraints and auxiliary losses promoting sparsity and disentanglement in the bottleneck layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for measuring and inducing composable abstractions under compute limits. It will yield insights into the relationship between environment curriculum, architectural bottlenecks, and zero-shot generalization. The resulting 'reportable rules' contribute to the alignment theme by fostering interpretability and verifying that the system captures intended principles.
proposals.novelty: The integration of dendritic gating mechanisms for creating modular bottlenecks within a multi-task RL agent trained on procedural curricula is novel. Combining this with an iterative preference learning objective to shape abstract representations moves beyond standard multi-task or meta-learning approaches prevalent before 2026.

proposals.title: Model Organism Learning: Isolating Core Learning Principles in Biologically-Plausible Micro-Architectures
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning systems are opaque, computationally expensive, and their scaling laws obscure fundamental algorithmic principles. Inspired by biology's use of model organisms like C. elegans, we propose a 'model organism learning' paradigm to study learning in isolation. This approach aims to uncover the minimal architectural and algorithmic components necessary for efficient learning and generalization, a critical step towards more aligned and interpretable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A drastically simplified neural network architecture, incorporating specific biologically-plausible mechanisms like apical dendrite-driven credit assignment, can learn to master procedurally generated 2D games, and the principles discovered will transfer effectively to slightly larger-scale problems.
proposals.method: We will design a micro-architecture (∼500 neurons) featuring segregated dendrites (basal for input, apical for top-down modulation) to implement a novel, local credit assignment rule. This network will be trained via deep reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO) within a minimal, procedurally generated 2D puzzle environment (e.g., a Sokoban variant). We will systematically ablate architectural components (recurrence, specific dendritic computations) and use analysis techniques from the researcher's recent work on iterative preference learning to interpret learning dynamics and failure modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new experimental framework for isolating and understanding core learning algorithms. It will produce concrete evidence for which minimalist architectural motifs are necessary for sample-efficient learning and generalization. The findings will provide a foundational, interpretable basis for scaling more efficient and aligned agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate extreme simplification of both architecture and environment to function as a 'model organism,' combined with the explicit focus on transferring discovered principles rather than achieving high performance. This contrasts with the dominant trend of scaling up opaque systems.

proposals.title: Generating Learnable Environments: Curriculum-Guided Game Design via Multi-Agent Self-Play
proposals.motivation: Automated environment design is a promising path toward open-ended learning and AI alignment, as seen in work on XLand and POET. However, current methods often generate environments that are either too random or too static, lacking a structured progression that efficiently teaches complex skills. This project addresses the need for environments that are not just novel, but are explicitly optimized for agent learning and generalization, creating a direct link between curriculum design and emergent complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: A curriculum that guides the co-evolution of game mechanics and agent policies via self-play will generate novel games that are inherently learnable and produce agents with stronger generalization capabilities than those trained on randomly or human-designed environments.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-level system. A 'Designer' module, using a language model or graph neural network, proposes game rule modifications constrained by a difficulty curriculum derived from machine teaching principles. A 'Player' population of agents, potentially using algorithms like MuZero or PPO with a dendritic network component for structured credit assignment, engages in self-play within the proposed game. Their learning progress, measured via regret or skill acquisition curves, provides a reward signal to the Designer via iterative preference learning (e.g., a learned reward model), closing the loop to evolve games that are optimally challenging.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for curriculum-driven, automated game design, 2) a new benchmark of procedurally generated games with known learnability properties, and 3) empirical evidence on how environment structure shapes agent generalization, offering insights for both AI training and educational game design.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit, algorithmic integration of machine teaching curricula as a direct objective for the environment-generating process, moving beyond novelty or diversity alone to optimize for the pedagogical quality of the generated environments.

proposals.title: Multi-Agent Self-Play with Apical Dendritic Credit Assignment for Emergent Social Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Self-play has driven breakthroughs in two-player zero-sum games like Go and chess, but real-world multi-agent systems (e.g., markets, diplomacy, social networks) involve three or more agents with complex, shifting alliances and competition. Pre-2026, scaling self-play to such many-player environments remains a significant challenge, crucial for developing AI that can navigate nuanced social and strategic interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending self-play with a novel credit assignment mechanism, inspired by apical dendrite function, will enable stable learning and the emergence of sophisticated social strategies in many-player, general-sum environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for N-player environments. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites for credit assignment, we will implement a gating mechanism where a top-down 'context' signal, derived from the joint action history and outcomes, modulates policy updates to disentangle individual agent contributions. This will be integrated with an iterative preference learning method, akin to Monte Carlo Tree Search-boosted reasoning, to refine policies from a dataset of multi-agent interaction trajectories, enabling agents to test and adapt social hypotheses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable algorithmic foundation for multi-agent self-play beyond two-player zero-sum settings. It will yield insights into the emergence of coalition formation, betrayal, and negotiation in AI systems, directly informing research on societal alignment and multi-agent cooperation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of biologically-inspired apical dendritic credit assignment with the self-play paradigm to solve the non-stationarity and credit assignment problems inherent in many-player games, moving beyond symmetric two-agent duels.

proposals.title: Apical Dendrite-Guided MCTS for Scalable Planning in High-Dimensional Action Spaces
proposals.motivation: Planning algorithms like Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) excel in discrete, low-dimensional action spaces (e.g., board games) but struggle to scale to the high-dimensional, structured action outputs required for real-world robotic control or language generation. This limitation prevents the application of robust, model-based planning to many modern foundation models and embodied AI systems. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing agents that can reason over complex, long-horizon tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neurally-inspired planning architecture, which uses apical dendrite-like mechanisms to learn and prune a latent action space, can guide MCTS to efficiently explore and plan in high-dimensional, continuous action domains.
proposals.method: We will adapt MCTS to operate over a learned, abstract latent action space generated by a foundation model policy. Inspired by recent work on apical dendrites for credit assignment, we will train a dendritic network module to predict the value of latent actions and their feasibility, pruning the search tree. This module will be trained via iterative preference learning on a dataset of successful and failed trajectories, refining its guidance. The full system will be tested in interactive environments requiring high-dimensional motor or language outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable planning algorithm for high-dimensional action spaces, directly extending the reasoning capabilities of MCTS. It will provide a concrete framework for integrating biologically-inspired neural computation with classical search, advancing the field of model-based reinforcement learning for complex agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a dendritic computation model for adaptive pruning within MCTS, moving beyond simple dimensionality reduction to an active, learnable mechanism for constraining search in structured, high-dimensional spaces—a non-obvious synthesis of neuroscience-inspired AI and planning.

proposals.title: Active Dendritic Networks and Monte Carlo Tree Search for Pixel-Based Mastery of Complex Strategy Games
proposals.motivation: Mastering complex strategy games like StarCraft II from raw pixels remains a grand challenge for AI, requiring the integration of visual perception, long-term planning, and strategic reasoning in vast state-action spaces. Pre-2026, methods often rely on privileged game-state APIs or are limited to simpler domains, failing to address the full perceptual and cognitive complexity a human player faces. Scaling to this setting is a critical step toward developing generally capable agents that can learn and plan from high-dimensional sensory inputs in open-ended environments.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent combining a biologically-inspired visual network with apical dendrite-like gating for selective feature modulation, and an iterative reasoning process guided by Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and preference learning, can achieve superhuman performance in complex strategy games directly from pixel input.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tiered architecture. First, a deep convolutional encoder with auxiliary dendritic compartments, inspired by cortical apical dendrites, will process game pixels to learn structured, task-relevant representations that can be dynamically gated for different strategic contexts. Second, this representation will drive a planning core using an adapted MCTS algorithm, where rollouts are evaluated by a reward model trained via iterative preference learning (as in recent LLM alignment work) on a dataset of expert and agent-generated trajectories. This closed-loop system will enable the agent to actively gather information and test strategic hypotheses within the game.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end agent capable of human-like strategic play from visual input, pushing the frontier of pixel-based RL. It will provide new insights into integrating structured planning with deep perception and demonstrate how alignment techniques, like preference learning from human feedback, can stabilize training in complex, multi-objective environments.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the co-design of an apical dendritic visual network for structured, context-aware perception with an MCTS planner refined through societal alignment frameworks, applied for the first time to the pixel-based mastery of a game as strategically deep as StarCraft II.

proposals.title: Inverse Dynamics in Latent Space: A Foundation for Active Hypothesis Testing and Alignment
proposals.motivation: Foundation models operating in interactive environments (e.g., robotics, embodied AI) require an understanding of how actions cause state changes to plan and test hypotheses effectively. Learning forward dynamics in a learned latent space is common, but the inverse problem—inferring the action that caused an observed transition—remains underexplored and is crucial for efficient reasoning and information gathering. This capability is foundational for the active hypothesis testing and societal alignment frameworks central to recent research.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to predict the action sequence responsible for a given transition between two latent states, derived from a pre-trained world model, will enable more sample-efficient planning and robust counterfactual reasoning for alignment. This inverse dynamics model will generalize better than pixel-space methods and provide a substrate for interpreting agent decisions.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pre-trained latent world model (e.g., using a variational autoencoder or transformer) that encodes observations into states and predicts forward dynamics. We will train an inverse dynamics network, potentially leveraging dendritic computation for separate integration of state and target information, to map a pair of consecutive latent states (s_t, s_{t+1}) to the action a_t. The model will be trained on trajectories from an interactive dataset, with performance evaluated using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) planning tasks, measuring the accuracy of inferred actions and the efficiency of plan generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, latent-space primitive for reasoning and planning, directly contributing to methods for active information gathering. It will offer a new pathway for alignment by making an agent's action rationale more interpretable within its learned world model. The resulting model can serve as a core component in iterative preference learning frameworks that require understanding action consequences.
proposals.novelty: While forward latent dynamics are well-studied, explicitly learning and utilizing a dedicated inverse dynamics model within a learned, abstract state space for high-level planning and alignment is a significant shift. The integration of this inverse model with MCTS for hypothesis testing and the use of dendritic architectures for processing state transitions are novel syntheses of pre-2026 concepts.

proposals.title: Synthetic Data for Agent Alignment via Model-Based Planning and Apical Dendrite Modulation
proposals.motivation: Training AI agents that are robust, sample-efficient, and aligned with complex objectives remains a central challenge in reinforcement learning. Real-world interaction for data collection is often costly, unsafe, or impractical, especially for testing alignment under diverse, long-horizon scenarios. Generating high-quality synthetic experience is a promising alternative, but current methods often lack the strategic depth and hypothesis-driven exploration needed for sophisticated alignment training.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned world model combined with a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) planner can generate high-value, imagined trajectories that, when used as synthetic training data, will improve the sample efficiency and final-task alignment of other agents, particularly when training targets apical dendrite signals for credit assignment.
proposals.method: We will train a foundational world model on a diverse dataset of agent interactions. An MCTS planner, guided by a reward function encoding an alignment objective (e.g., societal norms), will use this model to 'imagine' and score trajectories. These high-scoring synthetic trajectories will form a new training dataset. A separate 'student' agent, equipped with an apical dendrite-inspired architecture to modulate learning based on top-down objectives, will be trained on this data via offline reinforcement learning and iterative preference learning, refining its policy towards the planner's aligned solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-based pipeline for generating strategic synthetic data to bootstrap agent alignment. It will demonstrate how planning algorithms can actively test alignment hypotheses in simulation, and it will offer new insights into combining MCTS-derived data with biologically-inspired (apical dendrite) learning mechanisms for more efficient credit assignment in complex tasks.
proposals.novelty: While planning and synthetic data are known, their integration specifically for generating alignment-focused training curricula is under-explored. The key novelty is using an MCTS planner not for direct control, but as an automated data generator for training other agents, coupled with apical dendrite mechanisms to effectively leverage this planned, top-down information.

ideas.title: Semi-Supervised Reinforcement Learning via Bootstrapping with a Small Expert Seed
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the bootstrapping solution from computational biology, where limited experimental protein structures are used to seed a model that generates a vast, high-quality pseudo-dataset. Here, we explore a hybrid training paradigm for game-playing AI that combines a limited set of expert game data with self-play. The core method involves using the expert data to train an initial policy and a separate verifier model. The initial policy then generates trajectories via self-play. The verifier, acting as a proxy for expert judgment, filters these trajectories, selecting only those that align with expert-style play. These verified self-play outcomes are incorporated into the training data, effectively bootstrapping a larger, hybrid corpus from the small expert seed. This approach aims to accelerate learning, improve sample efficiency, and guide the search towards human-like strategies that pure self-play might overlook, while ultimately surpassing the expertise level of the original data.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Interactive World Model Learning for Self-Play
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how to structure self-play or autonomous exploration in environments where the rules or dynamics are initially unknown and must be learned interactively. The core idea is to co-learn a world model and a policy using a curriculum that is dynamically generated based on the agent's current estimated competence and the uncertainty of its model. The curriculum sequences learning challenges, starting from simple, high-probability transitions and progressively introducing more complex or uncertain regions of the state space, as guided by the evolving world model. This creates a synergistic loop where a better model enables a more informative curriculum, and a well-structured curriculum provides the focused experience needed to efficiently learn an accurate model and a robust policy.

ideas.title: Generative Game Design with High-Dimensional Action Spaces via Self-Play
ideas.description: This research combines the high-dimensional action space scalability of DDPG with the generative game design of self-play. The core idea is to frame the game design process itself as a high-dimensional, continuous control problem, where the agent's actions directly modify a vast array of game mechanics, physics parameters, and rule sets. Through competitive self-play between instances of this agent, the system can co-evolve both novel, complex game designs and policies capable of playing them, searching for designs that are not only balanced but also strategically deep and emergent due to their high-dimensional nature. This enables the automated creation of games with a level of mechanical nuance and complexity that would be prohibitively difficult for human designers to manually conceive and balance.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Automated Game Design via Self-Play
ideas.description: This research direction combines automated game design via self-play with curriculum learning principles from machine teaching. The core idea is to use a curriculum of increasingly complex game mechanics or rule constraints as the environment for self-play, where the agents' goal is to both master and co-evolve the game rules themselves. This process generates novel games that are guaranteed to have a structured difficulty progression and balanced emergent strategies, as they are born from a curriculum designed for learnability. The resulting games are not just novel and balanced, but are explicitly optimized to serve as effective training environments for AI agents, creating a virtuous cycle of game design and agent capability development.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Population Self-Play with Realism Priors for Complex Domains
ideas.description: This redesigned system tackles complex problems like robotics by implementing a multi-population adversarial framework where agent teams compete and collaborate. A central archive stores past policies, and a realism discriminator, trained on limited real-world data or high-fidelity simulations, scores interactions for physical plausibility. This creates a structured curriculum that prevents degenerate strategies, ensures the exploration of diverse and valid solutions, and grounds the self-play process in the constraints of the real world, leading to robust and generalizable policies.

ideas.title: Force learning from raw pixels via a perceptual bottleneck, inspired by sensory substitution
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fully observable state assumption by forcing the algorithm to learn from only raw pixels without auxiliary state features. It draws an analogy from neuroprosthetics and sensory substitution, where systems convert visual information into tactile or auditory stimuli that the brain learns to interpret as a functional perceptual model. The adapted solution is to create a 'perceptual bottleneck' for the RL agent by transforming raw pixel input into a different, information-preserving but unfamiliar sensory modality (e.g., edge maps, frequency-domain representations). The agent must learn to act based solely on this transformed perceptual stream, thereby forcing the development of robust internal state representations from foundational sensory features without relying on pre-defined state abstractions.

ideas.title: Model Organism Learning: Minimalist Architectures for Game Mastery
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from experimental biology's use of model organisms, this research adapts the 'scale down to fundamentals' approach to machine learning. In biology, complex systems are studied first in drastically simplified organisms (like C. elegans) to uncover core principles efficiently. We propose creating a 'model organism learning' framework: a drastically pared-down neural network architecture (e.g., a few hundred neurons in a novel, biologically-plausible micro-architecture) paired with a minimal, procedurally generated 2D game environment. The objective is to isolate and understand the fundamental computational and algorithmic ingredients necessary for learning and generalization—such as specific types of recurrence, attention, or credit assignment—within this ultra-low-resource setting on a single consumer GPU. Success is measured not by benchmark scores but by the transferability of discovered principles to slightly larger scales, establishing a foundation for efficient learning systems.

ideas.title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive System Identification
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the common RL assumption of a known, perfect simulator by drawing an analogy from control theory's system identification. In that field, engineers learn models of unknown physical systems through iterative interaction and observation. Adapted to RL, the agent would treat the environment as an unknown system to be identified. It would maintain and continually refine a dynamics model (e.g., a neural network or Gaussian process) based on interaction data. The core research problem becomes the dual objective of strategically exploring to improve the model's accuracy in uncertain regions while exploiting the current model for task performance, directly removing the need for a pre-defined, perfect simulator.

ideas.title: Adversarial Curriculum Learning via Ecological Niche Sampling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that self-play alone is optimal for training competitive agents. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where organisms adapt to diverse environmental pressures and competitors rather than just their own species, we propose a method called 'Adversarial Curriculum Learning via Ecological Niche Sampling.' The analogous solution from ecology involves maintaining biodiversity and exposure to varied challenges to prevent overspecialization and promote generalist robustness. Adapted to AI research, this involves curating a diverse, evolving ecosystem of opponent strategies—including the agent's past versions, strategically generated counter-strategies, human play data, and policies from other algorithms. The agent's training curriculum is then driven by sampling from this ecosystem to present maximally informative challenges. The research would investigate if this method improves learning speed, final robustness, and generalization compared to standard self-play, potentially by avoiding pathological equilibria and overfitting to a single, evolving opponent.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation to Novel Imperfect-Information Games
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-reinforcement learning framework where an agent is trained on a distribution of imperfect-information games (e.g., different poker variants, simplified Stratego configurations). The core innovation is that the meta-learning objective is to quickly adapt the agent's neural network components—specifically its policy, value, and crucially, its belief-state inference or opponent modeling modules—to a new, unseen game from the same family. This enables the agent to rapidly bootstrap competent play in a novel hidden-information setting by leveraging prior meta-knowledge about bluffing, information gathering, and reasoning under uncertainty, rather than learning everything from scratch.

ideas.title: Automated Machine Teaching for Adaptive Self-Play Curriculum
ideas.description: This approach integrates a machine teaching meta-controller with a self-play learning system. The teacher actively monitors the agent's performance and learning gradients to diagnose specific competency gaps. It then uses this diagnosis to synthesize or retrieve tailored training environments and opponent strategies that provide the maximal learning signal for the current learner state. This creates a fully adaptive, closed-loop curriculum where the training distribution is continuously optimized to accelerate learning and avoid plateaus inherent in pre-defined sequences.

ideas.title: Learning Composable Abstractions with Minimal Compute
ideas.description: This research designs a system that must learn fundamental, transferable abstractions from a highly constrained compute budget. Instead of training on single games, it uses a curriculum of procedurally generated game families that share core mechanics (like resource gathering or spatial navigation). The model architecture is forced to factorize knowledge through bottlenecks and modular components. Success is measured by zero-shot performance on unseen games from a held-out family and by the model's ability to explicitly report learned rules, ensuring the minimal system captures general principles rather than overfitting to superficial patterns.

ideas.title: Prior-Knowledge-Guided Model-Based Policy Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the hybrid DDPG-with-planning architecture with a systematic study of prior knowledge injection. The core idea is to construct a unified agent where prior knowledge—ranging from physical laws and symmetry constraints to approximate dynamics models or reward shapers—can be explicitly encoded into different components: the learned world model, the planner, or the policy/critic networks. By ablating or degrading the quality and quantity of this injected knowledge across controlled experiments, the work will establish a quantitative Pareto frontier, revealing how specific forms of prior knowledge most effectively compensate for a lack of real-world samples and which hybrid architecture (e.g., planning with a biased model vs. regularizing a model-free policy) best leverages each knowledge type.

ideas.title: Inferring Latent Game Structures and Social Dynamics from Multi-Agent Self-Play
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to analyze superhuman-level trajectories generated by multi-agent self-play in environments with three or more agents. The core challenge is to invert these complex, multi-lateral interactions to infer not only the explicit, underlying game rules but also the latent social structures and implicit conventions that emerge at scale. This goes beyond reconstructing a known game's rulebook to discovering the tacit 'rules of engagement'—such as temporary alliances, credible threats, or norms of communication—that the agents have learned are optimal. The outcome is a system that can explain the strategic fabric of sophisticated multi-agent ecosystems, providing a lens into emergent collective intelligence and complex social dynamics.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Skill Discovery with Intrinsic Sub-goals for Sparse Reward DDPG
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture to make DDPG viable in extremely sparse reward settings. The system first employs an unsupervised skill discovery module (e.g., using variational inference or empowerment maximization) to learn a repertoire of temporally extended actions from environment interaction, driven solely by intrinsic motivation. These learned skills form a hierarchy of sub-goals. A modified DDPG agent then uses a meta-controller to select skills and a sub-agent (the standard DDPG) to execute them, receiving dense intrinsic rewards for sub-goal achievement. The final performance is evaluated on the original sparse reward task, testing if this hierarchical decomposition can guide the policy to discover and chain necessary behaviors where monolithic DDPG fails.

ideas.title: Adaptive Normalization via Online Distribution Estimation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational statistics for handling unbounded streaming data to the challenge of unknown action ranges in RL. In statistics, online algorithms incrementally estimate the mean and variance of a data stream, enabling dynamic normalization without pre-defined bounds. Adapted to RL, the agent maintains and continuously updates running estimates of action distribution statistics (e.g., via Welford's algorithm for mean/variance or a robust alternative). These estimates are used to adaptively scale the policy's output or exploration noise, effectively creating a learned, data-driven normalization that removes the need for a pre-defined scaling function. This allows the algorithm to operate in unbounded action spaces, with the normalization co-evolving as the agent explores the environment.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Diverse Opponent Sampling with Core Self-Play Preservation
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid training regime that strategically blends learning from diverse opponents with a preserved core of self-play to maximize robustness and learning speed. The system maintains a dynamic archive of past agent checkpoints and external opponent strategies, from which it samples training partners according to a computed 'instructive value'. This value prioritizes opponents that have recently induced learning progress in the current agent, preventing distraction by irrelevant strategies. The core self-play thread ensures the agent does not forget the high-level strategic nuances necessary for peak performance, while the adaptive opponent curriculum efficiently fills strategic gaps and improves robustness.

ideas.title: Causal Rule Inference via Adversarial Perturbation of Play Objectives
ideas.description: This system addresses the fundamental identifiability problem in inverse reinforcement learning by generating and analyzing contrastive trajectory data. It trains an ensemble of agents under systematically perturbed or misspecified versions of the true game objective alongside a superhuman agent. By modeling the causal differences in the resulting play styles and outcomes, the framework can infer not just a set of rules consistent with optimal play, but the specific rules and objectives that best explain why suboptimal or divergent strategies fail. This turns the ill-posed inverse problem into a discriminative learning task grounded in counterfactual reasoning.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Knowledge-Efficiency Frontier in Hybrid Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research proposes a controlled experimental paradigm to quantify the precise relationship between the quantity and quality of prior knowledge (e.g., expert demonstrations, rules, or structured data) injected into a learning system and its subsequent sample efficiency and final performance in a hybrid self-play setting. Instead of simply testing 'with' or 'without' knowledge, the study would vary knowledge along multiple axes—such as volume, fidelity, and abstraction level—while measuring the impact on training stability, convergence speed, and asymptotic capability. The core objective is to derive a generalizable 'knowledge-efficiency frontier' that can inform optimal resource allocation between costly expert data collection and massive-scale autonomous learning for different problem domains.

ideas.title: Adaptive Trust-Region Policy Optimization for Unbounded Action Spaces with Safe Exploration
ideas.description: This approach enables learning in unbounded action spaces by concurrently learning a policy and a data-driven model of safe action bounds. It initializes with a conservative, small trust region for action outputs and uses exploration to gather evidence on the consequences of larger actions. The algorithm dynamically expands or contracts these empirical bounds based on the estimated gradient variance and the risk of state divergence, ensuring policy updates remain stable. This creates a robust, self-regulating system that can discover effective action scales without prior knowledge while avoiding the catastrophic failures of naive unbounded output.

ideas.title: Stabilized Recurrent DDPG via Decoupled Belief State Learning
ideas.description: This approach introduces a decoupled recurrent module that learns a predictive belief state from the history of observations and actions, trained with an auxiliary reconstruction or prediction loss. The actor and critic are feed-forward networks that receive this belief state as input, enabling them to handle partial observability without internal recurrence. By removing recurrence from the Q-learning loop, the method maintains the stability of original DDPG while effectively integrating memory, and includes a mechanism to regularly synchronize the belief state between behavior and target networks.

ideas.title: Robust Interactive Learning with Epistemic Uncertainty-Aware Beliefs
ideas.description: This approach learns an interactive game with unknown rules by explicitly maintaining a set of plausible world models, representing epistemic uncertainty. The agent's policy is conditioned on this belief state, prioritizing exploratory actions that reduce model disagreement and avoiding overconfident commitments to a single flawed hypothesis. This creates a system that learns efficiently while being robust to model misspecification and capable of safe, generalized performance under partial observability.

ideas.title: Learning from Diverse Opponents under Sparse and Delayed Adversarial Rewards
ideas.description: This research investigates the robustness of DDPG and similar off-policy actor-critic methods in competitive multi-agent environments where rewards are both sparse/delayed *and* generated exclusively through interactions with a diverse population of opponents, not a single self-play adversary. The core hypothesis is that a curriculum of opponents with varying strategies and skill levels provides a more structured and informative learning signal under extreme reward sparsity, helping to disambiguate the long-term consequences of actions. This tests whether diversity can compensate for infrequent feedback, leading to policies that are not only robust to sparse rewards but also generalize better to novel adversaries. The work challenges the sufficiency of self-play in sparse-reward settings and explores if population-based training is critical for learning temporally extended strategies when feedback is minimal.

ideas.title: Rule-Grounded Meta-Learning with Modular Hypernetworks for Robust Adaptation
ideas.description: This system combines meta-learning with a structured, rule-aware architecture to ensure robust adaptation to new game rules, even when they are partially out-of-distribution. A hypernetwork generates weights for a modular policy network based on a formal encoding of the game's rules, which provides a strong inductive bias. The hypernetwork is meta-trained on a diverse set of rule variations so that it produces networks that require minimal gradient steps for fine-tuning. An auxiliary module monitors adaptation performance and uncertainty to detect truly novel rules, allowing the system to fall back to a safe, general policy or seek external guidance, thereby preventing catastrophic failures.

ideas.title: Adapting Paleontological Stratigraphy for Sparse-Reward DDPG via Synthetic Intermediate Markers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of sparse or delayed rewards by drawing an analogy from paleontology, where scientists reconstruct timelines from extremely sparse fossil records using correlative markers like index fossils and chemical signatures. The analogous solution is to use widespread, identifiable markers to create a coherent narrative from fragments. Adapted to DDPG, the research would test robustness by augmenting environments with extremely infrequent rewards with synthetic, learnable intermediate markers—predictable changes in a latent state or achieved sub-goals. The agent learns to predict these markers, creating an internal, denser correlation structure that provides a proxy learning signal to guide policy and value function updates across long temporal gaps, effectively 'correlating' sparse reward events to improve learning efficiency and stability.

ideas.title: Adversarial Generalization Audit via Generative Game Spaces
ideas.description: This research moves beyond testing on a fixed, broader suite by first constructing a generative framework that parameterizes the space of possible games along fundamental axes like information structure and action dynamics. The core of the approach is an adversarial search procedure that actively probes this space to discover failure modes and performance cliffs for the algorithm under test. The outcome is a detailed 'generalization audit' map that identifies the algorithm's specific weaknesses and the structural game features that cause them, providing actionable insights for robust algorithmic improvement.

ideas.title: Neurosymbolic Rule Acquisition via Synthesis and Trace-Consistent Verification
ideas.description: This system learns game rules by synthesizing programmatic hypotheses that must satisfy a dual verification scheme. First, candidate programs are constrained by a high-level formal ontology of game concepts (e.g., turns, victory conditions). Second, each program is validated by executing it on a dataset of observed game traces and checking for consistency with a neural critic model trained to recognize rule-compliant and rule-violating states. This trace-consistent verification grounds the abstract programs in real gameplay data, ensuring the synthesized rules are both executable and semantically accurate to the observed dynamics, enabling robust generalization across rule sets.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Gated Hybrid Learning with Expert Data Priors
ideas.description: This system integrates limited expert data not as a fixed training component, but as a dynamic prior activated by uncertainty. A dual-head network estimates both policy and uncertainty, allowing the algorithm to identify states where self-play knowledge is tentative. In these high-uncertainty states, the learning objective blends with a constraint to mimic expert actions, providing guided exploration. This ensures expert knowledge directly shapes the exploration frontier, preventing both blind over-reliance on data and its complete neglect, leading to more sample-efficient and robust policy discovery.

ideas.title: Coalition-Robust Multi-Agent Training via Empirical Game-Theoretic Self-Play
ideas.description: This research develops a training framework for robust multi-agent learning in environments with three or more competing players. It addresses the instability of direct self-play by maintaining a population of agents and using empirical game-theoretic analysis to model the complex strategy space. Training proceeds via a curriculum that dynamically constructs adversarial scenarios, including temporary coalitions and betrayals, to pressure agents towards generally capable strategies rather than brittle equilibria. The core outcome is a system that produces agents robust to the shifting alliances and complex, non-transitive dynamics inherent in many-player competition.

ideas.title: Apply Progressive Overload and Periodization from Athletic Training to Self-Play Curriculum
ideas.description: This idea adapts the structured training methodology from sports science—specifically the concepts of progressive overload and periodization—to the problem of curriculum learning in machine self-play. In athletics, training is not a monotonic increase in difficulty but a planned sequence of phases targeting different physical and skill attributes, interspersed with recovery. The analogous solution is to design a self-play curriculum that functions similarly: it systematically plans phases where the opponent pool or environment complexity varies to apply targeted 'cognitive stress' on different strategic capabilities of the AI agent. The adapted research idea is to create a curriculum scheduler that, like a sports coach, periodizes training into distinct cycles (e.g., macrocycles and microcycles). These cycles would sequentially emphasize exploration, tactical refinement, and robustness, while strategically scheduling 'de-loading' phases against weaker opponents to solidify learning and avoid performance plateaus or instability. This moves beyond a simple complexity gradient to a dynamic, multi-dimensional training regimen inspired by biological adaptation.

ideas.title: Inferring Game Mechanics via Causal Structure Discovery from Trajectories
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a core problem in systems biology—inferring latent regulatory networks from observational data—to the original idea of deriving game rules from play. In biology, causal discovery algorithms (e.g., PC, FCI) are used to reconstruct gene regulatory networks from gene expression trajectories by analyzing statistical dependencies. Similarly, given superhuman play trajectories (sequences of states, actions, and rewards), we can frame the inverse problem as one of causal structure discovery. The adapted approach involves treating the trajectories as multivariate time-series data and applying constraint-based or score-based structure learning to infer a directed graphical model. This model represents the game's underlying mechanics: the causal relationships between game states, the conditions for legal actions, the sources of reward, and termination logic. The solution focuses on scaling these methods to handle the high-dimensional, partially-observed state spaces typical of complex games, thereby algorithmically 'reverse-engineering' the game's rules from optimal play behavior.

ideas.title: Adaptive PID Control for Continuous RL Action Spaces
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption of turn-based, discrete action spaces in RL by seeking adaptation for continuous or real-time decision-making. Drawing an analogy from Control Theory, specifically Industrial Process Control, where systems require smooth, continuous adjustments to variables in real-time—a problem solved by adaptive PID controllers. The analogous solution dynamically tunes proportional, integral, and derivative gains to produce stable, responsive control. Adapting this, the research idea proposes a hybrid architecture where a neural network policy, trained with a reinforcement learning algorithm like PPO or DDPG, does not output raw actions directly. Instead, it outputs the parameters for an adaptive PID controller (or a set of them for multi-dimensional action spaces). This PID controller then generates the final continuous action based on the current state error, providing an inductive bias for smooth and temporally extended actions, effectively relaxing the discrete, turn-based action assumption and enabling more natural real-time control.

ideas.title: Dual-Preference: Co-evolving game rules with human aesthetic priors
ideas.description: This system uses self-play not as the sole objective, but as one component of a multi-criteria optimization process for generating novel game rules. A learned model of human aesthetic preference, trained on human evaluations of procedurally generated rule sets, provides a critical reward signal to steer the search away from degenerate but algorithmically balanced games. The final process co-evolves a population of rule sets that are demonstrably balanced through self-play simulation, while also being scored as interesting, playable, and novel by the integrated human preference model.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Selective Feature Transfer with Negative Transfer Guard
ideas.description: This system pre-trains a modular feature network on a diverse set of source games. A lightweight, meta-learned gating controller is then attached, which is trained to predict the utility of each feature module for a new task. Upon encountering a new target game, the system uses a short probe phase to evaluate feature relevance via gradient alignment and task embedding similarity. The gating network then dynamically composes an active architecture, selectively enabling only the most beneficial pre-trained features while initializing others randomly, thereby preventing negative transfer and ensuring robust, accelerated learning.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Rule Synthesis via Competitive Co-Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the co-evolutionary framework from computational drug discovery to the domain of procedural game generation. The analogous problem is the search for novel molecular compounds with optimized pharmacological properties, solved by pairing a generative model with a predictive scoring function in a feedback loop. Here, we implement a 'Dual' system comprising a Rule Generator network and a Balance Predictor network. The Generator proposes novel game mechanics and victory conditions. The Predictor, trained on data from self-play simulations (e.g., win margins, decision entropy, comeback frequency), scores these rule sets for balance and interestingness. The highest-scoring games are fed back to train the Generator, creating a scalable, automated process for evolving entirely new, balanced, and strategically rich games without human-in-the-loop evaluation at each step. This mirrors the drug discovery pipeline, replacing molecular structures with game rules and binding affinity with gameplay quality metrics.

ideas.title: Applying Particle Filter Belief State Estimation to AlphaZero for Imperfect Information Games
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending the AlphaZero algorithm to imperfect information games like poker or Stratego by drawing an analogy from robotics and autonomous navigation. In robotics, particle filters are used to maintain a probabilistic belief state in partially observable environments, allowing an agent to plan effectively despite uncertainty. The adapted idea is to integrate a particle filter belief model into AlphaZero's architecture. The system would maintain a sampled set of possible true game states (particles) consistent with the observable history. AlphaZero's neural network would evaluate this belief distribution, and the Monte Carlo Tree Search would operate over these hypothetical branches, with action selection and value estimation averaging over the particle set. After each new public observation, the belief state would be updated via sequential importance resampling, focusing computational effort on the most likely true states. This approach aims to bridge the gap between perfect-information tree search and the hidden information domain, leveraging proven techniques for state estimation under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Minimalist Off-Policy Credit Assignment: Testing Sparse-Reward Robustness in a Computationally Constrained Sandbox
ideas.description: This research direction develops a minimalist, single-GPU experimental framework using small, procedurally generated grid-world games with tunable reward sparsity and delay. The core objective is to systematically deconstruct and evaluate the robustness of DDPG and similar off-policy algorithms to extremely sparse and delayed rewards in a controlled setting where compute is not a limiting factor for ablation studies. This allows for rapid iteration on algorithmic components like replay buffer sampling, critic bootstrap mechanisms, and exploration strategies specifically designed to bridge long temporal gaps. The result is a clear, interpretable sandbox for diagnosing credit assignment pathologies and validating lightweight improvements before scaling to more complex domains.

ideas.title: Generalize Algorithm Performance via a Systematic Game Taxonomy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational biology's CASP experiments to the problem of generalizing an algorithm's performance across games. The analogous field is Computational Biology, specifically protein structure prediction, where the Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction (CASP) established a systematic benchmark by creating a taxonomy of problem types and selecting representative targets for each. The adapted research idea is to move beyond ad hoc game selection by first developing a formal taxonomy of game characteristics (e.g., information structure, action space, reward profile). Then, construct a benchmark suite where each game is chosen as a canonical representative of a specific combination of these taxonomic dimensions. This approach provides a structured, interpretable, and comprehensive framework for testing algorithmic generalization, allowing researchers to pinpoint precisely which game features challenge the algorithm and which it handles robustly.

ideas.title: Hierarchical State Compression via Protein Folding Analogies
ideas.description: This research adapts the hierarchical solution to protein folding—a field confronting exponentially vast conformational spaces—to the challenge of scaling game-playing algorithms to state spaces far larger than chess or Go. The analogous solution from computational biology involves first predicting stable local structures (e.g., alpha-helices) to constrain the global search. Adapted here, a model is trained to recognize compressed 'strategic motifs' within the game, which serve as abstract, high-level actions. The core algorithm then searches over sequences of these motifs, effectively operating on a compressed representation of the state space, only drilling down into precise move sequences when necessary. This hierarchical compression enables exploration of games with branching factors previously considered intractable.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mechanism Design for Mixed-Motive Games via Meta-Game Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a framework where agents do not assume a fixed game type but actively infer the degree of alignment from interaction trajectories. The core algorithm is augmented with a mechanism that can propose and test social conventions or communication protocols, enabling coordination in partially observable settings. The system's meta-objective adapts, promoting individual learning in zero-sum phases and structured cooperation in mixed-motive phases, robustly handling the spectrum from pure competition to full cooperation. This creates agents capable of discovering and sustaining cooperative equilibria that would be inaccessible to fixed-policy learners.

ideas.description: This research direction develops a drastically scaled-down, single-GPU learning environment that applies a core multi-agent algorithm to small but strategically rich mixed-motive games, such as iterated matrix games with both competitive and cooperative elements. By minimizing computational overhead, it enables rapid, exhaustive experimentation across vast sweeps of game parameters, payoff structures, and agent architectures. This allows for the precise identification of the minimal algorithmic conditions necessary for the spontaneous emergence of cooperation, coalition formation, and norm development. The resulting lightweight testbed serves as a foundational probe for theories of cooperation before costly scaling, creating a new methodology for sample-efficient research in multi-agent alignment.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent Self-Play via Evolutionary Game Theory Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea extends the self-play paradigm to games with three or more competing agents by drawing an analogy from evolutionary game theory. The analogous problem of modeling multi-species co-evolution is solved using replicator dynamics and multi-population Evolutionarily Stable Strategies. The adapted research idea is to structure multi-agent self-play by maintaining distinct strategy populations for each agent role. Agents are sampled from these populations to interact in the many-player environment, and their success (fitness) dictates the replication and propagation of their strategies within their respective populations. This creates a system of co-evolutionary dynamics, scaling the self-play paradigm beyond two agents by providing a principled, population-based framework for training and stability analysis in complex, multi-competitive settings.

ideas.title: The Knowledge Gradient: Quantifying the Efficiency of Prior Information in Machine Learning
ideas.description: This research adapts the dose-response curve methodology from pharmacology to systematically study how injecting varying amounts of prior knowledge affects learning efficiency. In pharmacology, a dose-response curve quantifies the relationship between a drug's amount and its effect, identifying optimal therapeutic windows. Analogously, this study proposes constructing 'knowledge-response curves' for machine learning systems. The 'dose' is a quantifiable measure of prior knowledge (e.g., complexity of injected rules, strength of constraints), and the 'response' is a multi-faceted measure of learning efficiency. By running controlled experiments across different tasks and knowledge types, the research aims to empirically map these curves, identifying points of maximal efficiency gain, diminishing returns, and potential negative effects (over-constraint). This provides a rigorous, quantitative framework to challenge the 'zero knowledge' assumption and guide the principled integration of prior knowledge into learning systems.

ideas.title: Apply a Pharmaceutical Phase Trial Framework to DDPG Robotic Deployment
ideas.description: This idea proposes to deploy and test the DDPG algorithm on physical robotic systems by adapting the structured, risk-managed framework of pharmaceutical phase trials. The analogous solution from clinical pharmacology involves a multi-phase process (I-IV) to progressively test a new drug's safety and efficacy in increasingly broader and less controlled settings. Adapted to robotics, this means designing a 'Robotic Phase Trial' protocol: Phase I tests DDPG on a simple, safe physical task with constant supervision to establish baseline safety and hardware-software integration. Phase II introduces greater environmental variability and task complexity to evaluate learning efficacy and failure modes. Phase III involves deployment in the target operational environment, and Phase IV constitutes ongoing monitoring and adaptation post-deployment. This methodical approach mitigates the high risk and cost of direct real-world deployment, providing a clear pathway from lab validation to field operation.

ideas.title: AlphaZero-Lite: Reducing Computational Cost via Adaptive, Learnable Tree Search Pruning
ideas.description: This research develops a variant of AlphaZero that attacks the primary cost driver—the MCTS simulations—by making the search process itself more efficient and adaptive. A lightweight guiding network is trained concurrently to predict promising nodes, enabling aggressive pruning of low-value branches and focusing computational budget on critical decision points. The system dynamically adjusts the number of simulations per state based on position uncertainty, and employs distillation to keep the guiding network fast. This approach reduces total computation per training step while preserving or improving the sample efficiency of the policy and value learning updates.

ideas.title: A Causal Framework for Quantifying Inductive Bias and Its Effect on Learning Efficiency
ideas.description: This research establishes a controlled experimental paradigm to dissect how specific, quantifiable inductive biases—not vague 'prior knowledge'—affect learning dynamics. It defines a space of biases (e.g., spatial invariance, compositional structure, causal simplicity) and develops methods to inject them at precise strengths into learning systems. The core methodology involves systematically sweeping this bias space while measuring sample efficiency, generalization, and robustness, enabling the creation of a causal map from bias type and strength to learning outcomes.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecosystem Mutualism Models to Cooperative Game Theory
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from Theoretical Ecology to the original seed idea of applying a core algorithm to non-zero-sum or cooperative games. In ecology, mutualistic interactions (like those between plants and pollinators) are successfully modeled using modified Lotka-Volterra equations with positive interaction terms and network analysis, which quantify cooperative stability without assuming pure competition. The analogous solution involves identifying conditions and metrics for stable cooperation. Adapting this, the research would modify the core game-theoretic algorithm to incorporate variable alignment parameters and mutual benefit functions, transforming it from a purely competitive solver into a tool that can identify stable cooperative equilibria and Pareto-optimal joint strategies in games with mixed or aligned objectives, effectively treating cooperation as a modelable emergent property rather than an assumption.
